title_tesim: Santa Monica coastline from the Palisades Park cliffs at Montana Avenue, Santa Monica, 1946
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2889 P1 to 8. Santa Monica. 2 Palms – foot of Montana Ave. above Sorrento Club. 1946, 1952. Miss Carolyn Bartlett on view. Show file #.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2889 P 5

funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)
description_tesim: Photograph of the Santa Monica coastline from the Palisades Park cliffs, looking south.  At foreground, a large palm tree stands across from rock cliffs at left, a portion of the park’s rustic eucalyptus branch fence and trees in view. Below, a stretch of Pacific Coast Highway (California Highway 1), with cars lining the road and parked in lots near what are presumably beach homes, clubs and businesses. These structures follow along the Highway and beach- the beach populated by crowds on the sand and in the water. At center sits the Santa Monica Pier and Yacht Harbor. Beyond the pier, the coastline continues into the distance. Photographed at the foot of Montana Avenue, above the Sorrento Beach Club.

At some point after WWII the rustic branch fence at Palisades park was replaced with two types: a pre-cast concrete fence with a criss-cross design and a wood board fence. Bartlett photographs of Palisades Park dated 1946 show the rustic branch fence and the wood rail fence.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)

caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Old history. Arizona – California
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Original photograph by C.C. (Charles C.) Pierce. Caption on the photo notes the copyright date as 1902 by C.C. Pierce & Co. (photo numbered “1402”). Copy print date unknown.

Copy print photograph of a group of approximately 30 Mojave (Mohave) Indians (men, women and children) mourning over the body of their chief, Sistuma. They are seen seated and standing under the thatched roof of a porch attached to a small dwelling of clay construction (also with a thatched roof).  Additional members of the tribe can be seen next to one of several wood-frame and thatched structures viewed in the background.

title_tesim: Indigenous Africans conversing with a missionary, Central Africa, 1928-1948
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Central Africa. Natives & Missionary
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Copy photograph of villagers from an unidentified Central African nation, with Anglo missionaries. Men, women and children are represented in the photo; men and boys are seated on the ground with the missionaries, while the women and girls stand. The women wear wrappers (textiles wrapped around their bodies), and head ties. The men wear caftans and head coverings. They sit across from an Anglo wearing a pith helmet, shirt, pants, and boots. Behind the men sits a second Anglo, also wearing a pith helmet, shirt, tie and pants (probably rolled up) with tall socks. He sits atop a loose pile of thatch. Further behind him, a woman peaks out from part of a woven thatch structure. The group is surrounded by trees and foliage.

title_tesim: Sketches of the Yukon River, 1910
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1910. Yukon River. Copies of sketches.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Two color sketches representing the Yukon River’s varied landscapes. At top: Bartlett manipulates the natural landscape, creating a sky of dark and light green bands of color. A red ribbon floats in from above and out to the left of the image. The band is highlighted with violet. The pink and violet tines from above are reflected in the glaciers below. Bottom: The sun rises in a purple-hewn sky above the Yukon River, its red and gold rays radiating to the image edge and illuminating the floodplain at foreground. Adelbert Bartlett, artist.

title_tesim: Performers and attendees at the Will Rogers Memorial Celebration, Santa Monica, 1940
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2901, Will Rogers Celebration, Stage Show - Santa Monica July 25, 26, 27, year 1940.  Will's horse "Soapsuds," Leo Carillo.  At fence:  Tex Cooper in high boots & uniform, Leonard Trainor who takes care of horse, Linda Ware, actress, Carolyn Bartlett
Handwritten on edge of negative: 4
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Related to Los Angeles Times article, “Tribute Paid Will Rogers: Five Thousand Persons and 1400 Horses March in Santa Monica Parade Santa Monica Honors Rogers Hundred Thousand See Humorist's Favorite Horse in Parade,” 28 Jul. 1940

The Will Rogers Memorial Celebration and Pageant was a three-day event with proceeds going to the Red Cross. In 1940, the event coincided with the national convention of the Will Rogers Memorial Highway (U.S. 66) Association. Festivities included a tour of Will Rogers Ranch for convention delegates, a rodeo held at Municipal Stadium, and a nightly pageant held at Santa Monica High School.

Will Rogers Celebration stage show performers and attendees. At far left, Tex Cooper leans against a branch fence. He is wearing a uniform jacket with high boots and holds a wide-brimmed hat in one hand, the reins of Will Rogers’ horse “Soapsuds” in the other. The horse stands near him behind the fence. Leonard Trainor, Will Rogers’ former stunt double, rests a hand on the horse’s saddle. An unidentified man crouches on his knees next to the horse. A second group of men stand to the right. They include two unidentified performers, the event announcer, and Leo Carrillo. The announcer wears a contemporary suit, wingtip shoes and holds a panama hat, while Carrillo wears a contemporary overcoat with cowboy boots, a Stetson hat and scarf tie. The unidentified performers also wear western attire. One holds a guitar, wears a cowboy hat and boots, scarf tie and jacket; the other (holding his hat) wears a satin shirt and scarf. The crowd behind them is a mix of well dressed women and men in suits. They all stand in front of a painted forest backdrop. Probably photographed at Santa Monica High School.

description_tesim: Related to Los Angeles Times article, “Tribute Paid Will Rogers: Five Thousand Persons and 1400 Horses March in Santa Monica Parade Santa Monica Honors Rogers Hundred Thousand See Humorist's Favorite Horse in Parade,” 28 Jul. 1940

The Will Rogers Memorial Celebration and Pageant was a three-day event with proceeds going to the Red Cross. In 1940, the event coincided with the national convention of the Will Rogers Memorial Highway (U.S. 66) Association. Festivities included a tour of Will Rogers Ranch for convention delegates, a rodeo held at Municipal Stadium, and a nightly pageant held at Santa Monica High School.

A crowd at the Will Rogers Celebration stands behind an announcer seated at a desk. He holds a standing microphone with one hand. Lee Carrillo (in overcoat and hat) stands left of young woman in a satin dress with a buttoned ruffled front and puffed sleeves. To their far right, a boy on a pony wears a cowboy hat and boots. A woman stands next to him holding the horse’s reins. She wears a Plains Indian tunic dress and headband. With exception of a few children, members of the crowd are a mix of well-dressed women and men in suits, many wearing various styles of hats. Probably photographed at Santa Monica High School.

title_tesim: “Lucia di Lammermoor” scene with Natalie Garrotto performed during “One Night at the Opera,” Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1950
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Nite [sic] of opera. 163 to 170.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Excerpt from “Lucia di Lammermoor” was performed as part of “One Night at the Opera,” an event showcasing scenes from the Santa Monica Civic Opera’s most popular operas. The performance took place January 28, 1950 at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

Opera singers performing Act 2, Scene 2 from “Lucia di Lammermoor.” Lucia (Natalie Garrotto) wears a wedding gown and veil and is being comforted by her handmaid, Alisa (Catherine Mazet). Lucia turns away from Edgardo, laird of Ravenswood (portrayed by Ray Gagan) as he sings. He wears a button-down shirt, fitted breeches and tall boots. He also carries a sword at his side. Not far from him stands Enrico, laird of Lammermoor (Enrico Porta), who wears a satin doublet with lace-trimmed collar, breeches and stockings. Additionally, cast members in the role of nobles and courtiers watch as events transpire. June Moss (second from left) performs as one of these nobles. A fabric covered table topped with a quill pen and ink set are the only visible stage props.

title_tesim: "Lucia di Lammermoor" "mad scene" with Natalie Garrotto performed during “One Night at the Opera,” Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1950
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Nite [sic] of opera. 163 to 170.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Opera singer Natalie Garrotto in the title role, performing the “Mad Scene” from “Lucia di Lammermoor.” Garrotto stands center stage while cast members, dressed as 17th century nobles, watch events unfold. The setting is a hall in Lammermoor Castle. Upstage, a stair case and narrow wall panels suggest a grand room. Downstage, two chairs and a table topped with quill pen and ink are the only props visible. Musicians are viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground. Additionally, June Moss (standing left of the staircase) performs as a noble in attendance.

The excerpt from “Lucia di Lammermoor” was performed as part of “One Night at the Opera,” an event showcasing scenes from the Santa Monica Civic Opera's most popular operas. The performance took place January 28, 1950 at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

title_tesim: "Pagliacci" production with Enrico Porta, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1955
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 24
Text from negative sleeve: [?] to 25. SM Opera. Cavalleria & Pagliacci. 1955. June Moss.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Pagliacci, a drama in two acts, was performed in a double-bill with Pietro Mascagni’s opera, Cavalleria Rustica, a melodrama in one act.

Opera singers performing in Ruggero Leoncavallo’s, Pagliacci. Children from the audience sit onstage with cast members, making up the audience for a play-within-a-play. They watch along with Nedda (playing Columbina) as Enrico Porta, playing Tonio (playing Taddeo, holding a basket) sings. She stands on a secondary stage resembling a Greek temple. He stands in front on the primary stage. The secondary stage is covered by a canopy with drapery; a drum is propped against the frame. A banner painted with “I Pagliacci” is attached to the pediment. Musicians in the orchestra pit can be seen in the immediate foreground. Performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
A notice for this production, conducted by Mario Lanza and performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 7 Aug. 1955: E7

title_tesim: "Cavalleria Rusticana" production, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1955
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 19
Text from negative sleeve: [?] to 25. SM Opera. Cavalleria & Pagliacci. 1955. June Moss.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: A notice for this production, conducted by Mario Lanza and performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 7 Aug. 1955: E7.

Opera singers performing in Pietro Mascagni’s, Cavalleria Rusticana. Cast members are gathered center stage in Italian peasant costumes while a singer, portraying Alfio, stands to the fore of the group holding a whip. Behind the players, an Italian village is depicted using a painted backdrop and set replete with a wine shop and church. Musicians are visible in the orchestra pit at foreground. Performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
Cavalleria Rustica, a melodrama in one act, was performed in a double-bill with Ruggero Leoncavallo’s opera, Pagliacci, a drama in two acts.

title_tesim: Painting of Ruth St. Denis, modern dance pioneer, in Southeast Asian attire, (painting, 1912)
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Ruth St. Denis 1912. Photo of painting.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of a framed painting of Ruth Sr. Denis depicted wearing a sari and choli (short blouse). She looks over one shoulder and holds a dish in one hand, the other hand is held up over head with the palm up. Painting signed, “Walter Barron Currier, California, 1912.”
Date of painting is 1912; date of photograph unknown.

Ruth St. Denis was a modern dancer who incorporated eastern dance into her work. Walter Barron Currier was a Massachusetts-born painter, craftsman, illustrator, print maker and etcher. He moved to the Los Angeles area in 1913. Currier was the head of Lincoln High School's art department, and established the Currier Creative Art School in Santa Monica in 1926.

Cavalleria Rustica, a melodrama in one act, was performed in a double-bill with Ruggero Leoncavallo’s opera, Pagliacci, a drama in two acts.

description_tesim: Singers in a Santa Monica Civic Opera production of Giacomo Puccini’s, La Bohème, perform in period costumes. Singers in the roles of Schaunard, Mimi and Rodolfo stand stage-left, while singers portraying Musetta and Marcello perform center stage. The painted backdrop and set props recreate an attic apartment in Paris, including a prominently displayed painting, a table and covered settee. Windows painted with snow-covered panes suggest the winter season outdoors.  Musicians are visible in the orchestra pit at foreground. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

description_tesim: This image is of a group of Santa Monica College students working on the school paper, the SaMoJaC. Some students sit at desks with typewriters before them. Another student points to the large image of a mock front page plastered on the building behind them. Still others stand with books and papers in their hands.
The SaMoJac was first printed in 1929, and would change its name to the Corsair in 1945. E.R Coulson was a professor at Santa Monica College who oversaw the printing of the paper in the late 1930s.

title_tesim: Andrea Karlsen and Paul Maure, directors of the Ballet des Arts Company, photographed by Constantine, copy print 1964
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1964. Ballet La Canada. Music Arts.

Text from newspaper caption: Ballet Stars - Andrea Karlsen and Paul Maure will be featured when the Ballet des Arts at La Canada opens the Symphonies-by-the-Sea season in Santa Monica High School on Friday, July 17 ["La Canada Dancers Will Appear at Beach," Los Angeles Times, 12 Jul. 1964: WS4].
Handwritten on photograph: Constantine
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Andrea Karlsen, a native of Los Angeles,  began her dance studies in New Zealand with Estelle Berre, then studied in Los Angeles  with Bronislava Nijinska. After WWII, Nijinska took her to France to join Le Grand Ballets du Marquis de Cuevas, where she attained principal dancer status in 1953. She remained with the company until 1955.

The caption on the photograph, "Constantine," refers to the photographer Constantine Hassalevris. When an automobile accident ended his career as a professional dancer, Hassalevris became a photographer. He photographed Danilova, Dolin, Eglevsky, Lichine, Markova, Massine, Slavenska, Tudor, Toumanova and Zorina, among others and was later the photographer for Dance magazine.
Photograph of ballet dancers Andrea Karlsen and Paul Maure in costume by the photographer Constantine. Maure kneels as  Karlsen holds an arabesque pose behind him with one hand on his shoulder.

Paul Maure was a dancer with the Ballet Russe de Monte Carlo, Le Grand Ballet du Marquis de Cuevas and the Folies Bergere. He and his wife, Andrea Karlsen, were the directors of the Ballet des Arts Company in La Canada.

title_tesim: Farm workers and plow with Fontana founder A. B. Miller (possibly), Fontana, 1928-1931
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2098… 2102–2103–2104–2105–2106–2107–2108–2109–2110–2111–2112. Fontana. Chickens – Corrals. [stamped:] Adelbert Bartlett Los Angeles
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2102
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

description_tesim: Fontana was founded in 1913 by A.B.  Mille. Miller first purchased the 17,000 acres of land in 1906, a year after he began working it. Miller envisioned a booming agricultural town complete with citrus orchards, cattle and poultry farms situated along Route 66. With the formation of the Fontana Land Company in 1914 and Fontana Farms Company in 1918, Miller’s vision began to come to fruition. The Los Angeles Times printed numerous articles regarding A.B. Miller, Fontana Farms and Fontana Land companies from 1914 to 1937; articles specific to poultry farming and egg production date from 1925 to 1931.

Two men stand on a stretch of gravel holding caving prongs. The man on the left, wearing a three-piece suit and tie, hat and glasses, may be A. B. Miller. The man on the right wears a work shirt, pants and a hat. He is standing next to a large piece of covered farm equipment (probably a plow). A man sitting inside the apparatus also wears work clothes, a hat and gloves, while a young girl stands atop a mechanism below him, posed hand on hip. Groves of trees fill the background. Photographed in Fontana.

title_tesim: Political cartoon featuring the "General Pete Monkey Circus," commentary on Santa Monica Bay drilling, 1954
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Cartoons, 1954. Oil. Montana Ave Laundries. Oil.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The cartoon references “Gen’l Pete” – shorthand for General Petroleum Corporation. In 1954, a contractual agreement was made between General Petroleum and the Santa Monica City Council in which the oil concern was granted a 10-year lease on Santa Monica tidelands. General Petroleum offered the city $500,000 and 35% royalties from recovered oil. The contract was dependent on a voter referendum that would change pre-existing city ordinances, thus allowing for oil drilling in city-owned tidelands. The amendment proposal lost by a 3:1 margin. In addition, Bartlett references the Civic Auditorium (on the building at top right), likely referring to a $2,895,000 bond issue also up for referendum. The bond was approved by voters.
For duplicate drawing see image ark no. 21198.zz002d5q93

Photograph of a political cartoon drawn by Adelbert Bartlett. The drawing lampooning General Petroleum and Santa Monica city management is set within the “Gen’l Pete Monkey Circus.” A building reminiscent of a fun house is depicted at the top, under text reading: “Voters! Think Twice Before You Vote Tues. Nov. 2.”  The words, “Civic Aud[itorium]” and “Town Hall” are written at top. Speech bubbles emanate from the mouth of a monkey’s head looming above the entrance, stating, “We’re Goners- -Jump!” and “Divy up Oil Bonus.”  Monkeys in top hats and suits run out of the building with “Imperium II” written above its doors. Their arms are outstretched in the air and one is depicted performing a cartwheel. At right, the City Manager floats through the air, buoyed by a parachute. Above him is a large bag of money. The circus balances perilously on the back of one man, bent over, his torso covered by a barrel. He holds a balancing pole while walking a tight rope strung between the Palisades cliffs and a “mythical oil pool” surrounding oil derricks in the bay. Seated under a palm tree near the cliff’s edge is a figure playing an instrument. Text beneath the figure: “Pete’s Horntooter O Delusion,” followed by the emphatic statement, “Oil . Vote No.” The Pacific Coast Highway and other landmark structures are depicted at bottom, with the bay and coast line captured at center. The cartoon is signed “BART” in the left-hand corner.

title_tesim: “Cavalleria Rusticana” production with Florence Korsak, Ruth Ralston and Enrico Porta, Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles, 1951
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 16
Text from negative sleeve: Pace Operas. Cavalleria and Pagliacci. Pace 12/10/51. #15 to 28/29. Nelda.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Production of “Cavalleria Rusticana,” performed by the Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera. Groups of male and female cast members, in Italian peasant costume, congregate and converse in the background. To the fore, near a church façade, stands Alfio (Enrico Porta) with whip in hand. Standing in opposition is Mamma Lucia (Ruth Ralston). Seated at the table near her is Santuzza (Florence Korsak). The setting is a 19th century Sicilian village. Painted backdrops and sets depict stone and brick architecture surrounding a village square. Beyond the stage, a musician is viewed in the orchestra pit, while an American flag drapes along a column at left. Opera performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles.

Leoncavallo’s “Pagliacci” and Mascagni’s “Cavalleria Rusticana” were performed as a double bill at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, December 10, 1951. The opera was produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace. A critique of the production appears in a Los Angeles Times article, “’Cavalleria’ Given at Wilshire Ebell,” 11 Dec. 1951: B9.

title_tesim: "Ruddigore" production, (North Hollywood, prpbably), 1956
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 7
Text from negative sleeve: Oct '56. Mary Jesson, Valley opera, Ruddigore.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

description_tesim: Mary Jesson, born Ethel Mary Jesson (1917-2006), was a performer with the Valley Savoyards and president of the Gilbert and Sullivan Society (affiliated with the D’Oyly Carte Company and various Gilbert and Sullivan performance groups), as noted in the Pasadena Independent, 19 Dec. 1966:11.

Formed in 1956, the Valley Savoyards opera company was devoted to performing works by Gilbert and Sullivan. (Devotees of Gilbert and Sullivan are called "Savoyards" after the Savoy Theatre in London, which was built specifically for the presentation of the pairs' operas). Notices for Valley Savoyards performances appear in the Los Angeles Times for only two years (1959-1960), with performances at locations in North Hollywood (most notably at the Assistance League Playhouse), and one performance at the Encino Community Center.

Opera singers performing in a Valley Savoyards production of "Ruddigore." Mary Jesson, performing as Rose Maybud, stands at left and watches a male and female performer perform at the fore.  Jesson and several of the female cast members hold floral bouquets and wear bridal attire that includes long, light-colored dresses and veils. The male cast members wear costumes evoking the 19th century - top hats, tail coats, waistcoats (vests), and shirts with lace ruffs and cuffs. Photographed on stage at an unidentified theatre, likely in North Hollywood (Los Angeles) or an adjacent San Fernando Valley locale.

title_tesim: Political cartoon commenting on Congressional Representative Mark S. Hogue, 1954
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Cartoons, 1954. Oil. Montana Ave Laundries. Oil.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

description_tesim: For duplicate drawing see image ark no. 21198.zz002d5pxx

Reverend Mark S. Hogue was elected as 16th District Congressional Representative on November 2, 1954. He was endorsed by the International Longshoremen’s and Warehouseman’s Union, which was headed by Harry Bridges. Bridges was identified as a member of the Communist Party by several witnesses appearing before government agencies and investigating committees.

Photograph of a political cartoon drawn by Adelbert Bartlett. The drawing shows a man walking along a path. At left, a griffin flies over head with “Left-Wing” printed on its wing. Farther along the path at right is the depiction of an implied crucifixion (as illustrated by “Golgotha” written above three inferred crosses). The cross at center is being constructed with the help of a rope and pulley – the pulley being a diamond pendant. Beneath the cross is a posted flyer referring to Rev. Mark S. Hogue, 16th District Representative, and Democrat. The bill pronounces: “Hogue, yesterday’s candidate, unwitting pawn, [un-American] factions, unsound advice, untenable promises. What is a man profited. if he gain the whole world. yet lose his own soul…?” [Text at bottom:] “16th congressional position already filled. [Text at left:] Coming Election Day. The Sacrifice. Vote early.”

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This image is of a group of Santa Monica College students working on the school paper, the SaMoJaC. Some students sit at desks with typewriters before them. Another student stands next to a mock front page plastered on the building behind him, with papers in his hands. The SaMoJac was first printed in 1929, and would change its name to the Corsair in 1945. E.R Coulson was a professor at Santa Monica College who oversaw the printing of the paper in the late 1930s.

title_tesim: “Pagliacci” production with Julian Oliver, Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles, 1951
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Pace Operas. Cavalleria and Pagliacci. Pace 12/10/51. #15 to 28/29. Nelda.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Production of “Pagliacci,” performed by the Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera. Cast members dressed as Italian villagers look on as Pagliacci, portrayed by tenor Julian Oliver in black and white clown costume, sings. He is flanked by two unidentified cast members. The setting is a 19th century Calabrian village. Costumes and scenery flats evoke the time and place: women wearing full skirts peasant blouses; men wearing breaches, short jackets and sashes around their waists. The performers are surrounded by trees, suggesting an outdoor, country landscape. To the right stands a secondary stage resembling a Greek temple with comedy and tragedy masks painted on the pediment. The stage’s interior is obscured by curtains. Beyond the stage, musicians can be viewed from the orchestra pit at foreground.  Opera performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles.

Leoncavallo’s “Pagliacci” and Mascagni’s “Cavalleria Rusticana” were performed as a double bill at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, December 10, 1951. The opera was produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace. A critique of the production appears in a Los Angeles Times article, “’Cavalleria’ Given at Wilshire Ebell,” 11 Dec. 1951: B9.

description_tesim: Opera singers in a performance of “Cavalleria Rusticana.” Cast members wearing Italian peasant costumes line up across the stage and sing with Turrido (portrayed by Julian Oliver in velvet breeches, short jacket, and sash at the waist) and Lola (Verna Montreys in full skirt, peasant blouse and waist cincher). The characters raise their glasses at a table set outside of the cantina (out of view in this photo).  The setting is a 19th century Sicilian village. Painted backdrops and sets depict stone and brick architecture surrounding a village square. A church figures prominently, standing opposite a cantina (out of view in this photo).  Beyond the stage set, an American flag drapes at left of camera view.  Opera performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles.

description_tesim: Louise Glaum produced and directed “O.K. By Me,” a comedy written by Sheldon Shedard. Glaum was an actress, drama teacher and director who was active in the Los Angeles area for over 60 years. Originally known as one of the first “vamp” actresses, she later opened several theaters in Los Angeles and an acting school.

Cast members from the play “O.K. By Me,” onstage in a dress rehearsal. Cast from left to right: Stanley Herbert Briggs, Judith Evans, Otis Garth, Pearl Jenkins (in blackface), Peggy O’Connell, Margaret Wells, and Wally Norman.
This production was referenced in two Los Angeles Times articles: “Stage Farce Offered at Beaux Arts Theater,” 11 Oct. 1952, and “Beaux Arts Will Reopen,” 19 Sep. 1952.

title_tesim: Political cartoon commenting on Montana Avenue laundries, Santa Monica, 1954
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Cartoons, 1954. Oil. Montana Ave Laundries. Oil.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of a political cartoon drawn by Adelbert Bartlett. At center, bundles of laundry sit on shelves with “Will Call” printed above. To the left, a spinning vortex (perhaps a washing machine) appears to create soap suds. Machines sit just to the left in the background. The image is surrounded by text. Text reads: “-Mat’n –Ett. Sudsy Monkey Business. Montana Avenue Laundries. ‘You Bring – We Do’ – Commercial – They Violate Self-Service Permit. Must We Submit to Spot Favors, ‘See Me’ Zoning, Downgraded Home Values? ...Montana (Laundry) Avenue. Today’s Road of Out-Zone Laundries- Animal Breeders – Heavy Machine Shop – Heavy Auto Repairs – Home Owners: Protest to Your City Council – Press – Growing Industrial Invasion & Ruin Home Areas.”
For duplicate drawing see image ark no. 21198.zz002d5q8k

title_tesim: Political cartoon commenting on Montana Avenue laundries, 1954
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Cartoons, 1954. Oil. Montana Ave Laundries. Oil.

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of a political cartoon drawn by Adelbert Bartlett. At top, an image of monkeys (one male and one female) depicted folding laundry. They are flanked by text, music notes and soap suds. Text reads: “Laundry Lane by ‘Sudsy’.” At bottom, bundles of laundry sit on shelves with “Will Call” printed above. To the left, a spinning vortex (perhaps a washing machine) appears to create soap suds. Machines sit just to the left in the background, the words, “Now Sudsy Laundry Lane” are written above them with music notes. Remaining text reads: “Montana Avenue Laundries. ‘You Bring – We Do’ – Commercial – They Violate Self-Service Permit. Must We Submit to Spot Favors, ‘See Me’ Zoning, Sudsy Laundry Lane.” [At bottom:] “Home Owners: Protest to Your City Council – Press – Growing Industrial Invasion & Ruin Home Areas.”
Cartoon is an elaborate variation of another drawing. There are two copies (see image ark nos. 21198.zz002d5q2g and 21198.zz002d5q8k)

Text from negative sleeve: 2907. Yukon Society Convention 1949. Wm H Brown L.A.
named_subject_tesim: Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: International Sourdough Reunion, Inc. is a non-profit association of fraternal organizations that share the pioneer history of Alaska and the Yukon in common. Each year, one organization hosts a convention of members from groups located as far north as Fairbanks, Alaska, and as far south as San Diego, California (with points in between). In 1949, the Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California hosted the 18th annual International Sourdough Reunion convention. Events were held at the Hotel Alexandria in Los Angeles. The four-day event (August 17-20) was noted in the article, "Gold Rush is On: Tales of '98 Relived at Sourdough Meeting," Los Angeles Times, 18 Aug. 1949: A2.

Men and women, wearing suits and ties or dresses, standing behind a length of tables set aside for designated speakers at the 18th annual International Sourdough Reunion banquet, hosted by the Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California, Hotel Alexandria, Los Angeles.
combined_subject_ssim: Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California

Several wear leis around their necks, while two wear party hats. One woman (seated fourth from right) wears an "Alaska" badge on her chest.

Included in the photo are presumed family members that are featured in three additional images. They include a man standing with the child wearing western attire (seen along the wall at right), and three females seated facing the camera at left. (See image ark nos. 21198/zz002d5zt7, 21198/zz002d5zvr, 21198/zz002d5zw8, and 21198/zz002d606v)
combined_subject_ssim: Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California

title_tesim: Ribbonwood trees on a mountain path, Ribbonwood resort, Santa Rosa Mountains, Mountain Center vicinity, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2803. Ribbonwood Resort. San Jacinto Mts Via Keen Camp Post Office. Wn S. Howell Jr. proprietor. Photos of Ribbonwood Trees, 10/1/37. Dr. St. Louis Estes. Jean Myras.
Handwritten along edge of negative: 2803 P…
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Santa Rosa and San Jacinto Mountains National Monument (Calif.)
description_tesim: Photograph of ribbonwood trees along a path in the Santa Rosa Mountains. Photographed on the property of the Ribbonwood resort.

The Ribbonwood resort, located at the base of the Santa Rosa Mountains, was owned and operated by W.S. (Wilson Stout) Howell, Jr., d.1888-1967. Howell purchased 2000 acres of land at the base of the Santa Rosa Mountains from the Southern Pacific Railroad with the intention of farming, but when the Pines-to-Palms Highway opened in 1932, he decided to open a rest stop to capitalize on the traffic that ran through portions of his property. Soon after, he began to clear paths to trails leading up to the Santa Rosa Mountains and created picnic areas. Rustic-style cabins soon followed. By 1935, Howell’s land was a full-fledged resort. Howell’s intention was to offer a place where those in ill health could rest and recuperate in a natural environment. He believed that the mountain location helped him rebuild his health and that it could do the same for others. Howell sold his land in 1964. It was developed into the town of Spring Crest, which sits within the Santa Rosa – San Jacinto Mountains National Monument.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Rosa and San Jacinto Mountains National Monument (Calif.)

subject_tesim: Santa Monica Canyon (Los Angeles County, Calif.)
description_tesim: Photograph of an unidentified man (in suit and tie, holding a smoking pipe) and seated women in a room with Egyptian furnishings. Both are photographed in quarter profile. They appear to be speaking and listening to a third person out of view regarding the double seat bench she is sitting on. The bench is ornately carved and inlaid with mother of pearl and (probably) bone. Behind the seating sits a metal lamp on a short side table (partly visible on the right). It is hand-tooled with geometric shapes and floral-patterned cutouts, and decorated with strands of beads. Two wall sconces can also be seen behind the couple. An Oriental rug is viewed on the floor at foreground next to a potted plant.

title_tesim: Typewritten description of an accident with a Hollywood Cab Co. driver, 1953
caption_tesim: Text reads in part: Film negative hold. Jan. 14, 1953. Photographed by A. Bartlett at Hollywood Cab stand, SM Blvd on or about noon, Jan. 14, 1953. Hollywood Cab Co. license plate BE X 78938, liquor delivery card on rear, driven by Carl Milton Betz, 133 N. Orange Gr., B l, drivers license issued until 3-27-54 (driver did NOT have a license with him on run in late forenoon Jan 14, 1953, at Doheny Dr. and Cynthia St., Beverly Hills, when the above named car side-swiped at the moment of impact Pakard [sic] convertible, 37-6, which was close to center white line. No appreciable damage done to either car. Obvious old scratches and dents on both cars. Cause of accident, Cab Co. driver said immediately thereafter he misunderstood signal of Packard driver as being signal for left turn. This is a narrow street, and construction cars and trucks were parked well away from curb on right hand side of Doheney Dr. thus making this street even more hazardous for passing on right side of car going in same direction. No police officers present. Desk men at Sheriff's office on S.M. Blvd/ also State Highway Patrol at 11178 S.M. Blvd. West LA declined to accept filing of any accident report by Bartlett, Packard driver.

title_tesim: Calico ghost town cemetery, San Bernardino County, Barstow vicinity, copy print, 1929
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2590. Calico Mine. Mojave Desert, 1929.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Copy print photograph of a cemetery at Calico mining camp. Rock mounds with stakes delineate some of the grave sites, and three have picket fence structures (one with a shingled roof) remaining.

Located in the Calico Mountains of the Mojave Desert, the town of Calico was the largest silver mining camp in California. The mine operated from 1881 until it was abandoned in 1896. The town remained largely deserted but for a few who remained until the end of borax mining in the region in 1907. Today, Calico is a State Historic Landmark (#782), and is a designated San Bernardino County Regional Park.

title_tesim: “Carmen” production with June Moss and Catherine Mazet, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1956
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 15
Text from negative sleeve: 1956. SM Opera. “Carmen” 10/13/56. June Moss.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in Act 1 of “Carmen.” Catherine Mazet and June Moss (standing third and fourth from left, first row) and fellow cast members wear Andalusian attire (men dressed in the vaquero style wearing short jackets or vests and breeches with wide sashes and wide-brimmed hats; women wearing head scarves, peasant blouses and full skirts with fabric tied at the waist) watch as a singer portraying the character Carmen sings to the men surrounding her. Don José, a soldier, sits to the left of stage with sword at side. He appears disinterested in the crowd’s activity. At left stands a scenery flat representing a tobacco factory.  A second flat representing a guard house stands across the stage. The setting is a town square in Seville, Spain. A backdrop painted with additional buildings and an open plaza creates the appearance of a town square. Photographed from the audience, the conductor and musicians in the orchestra pit and audience members in view. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
A notice for this production appears in the Los Angeles Times, 14 Oct. 1956: E6.

title_tesim: “Carmen” production, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1956
caption_tesim: Handwritten on negative edge: 16
Text from negative sleeve: 1956. SM Opera. “Carmen” 10/13/56. June Moss.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in Act 4 of “Carmen.” Cast members wearing Andalusian attire – women wearing mantillas and holding hand fans; men wearing short jackets and wide brimmed hats – stand in the background as singers portraying the characters Carmen and Escamillo perform downstage center. Escamillo wears a matador’c costume.  The setting is a bullring façade with authentic posters from bullfights at Plaza de Toros de Castellon, Plaza de Toros de Gijon, and Plaza de Toros de Granada. A 1951 date can be seen on two of the three posters. Photographed from the balcony, the conductor and musicians in the orchestra pit and audience members in view. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
A notice for this production appears in the Los Angeles Times, 14 Oct. 1956: E6.

Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in Act 3 of “Carmen.” Female cast members wear Romany (Gypsy) attire – head scarves, peasant blouses, full skirts and shawls. Most of the men are dressed as bandoleros, wearing cloaks and bandanas around the head. They watch as singers portraying Don José (a soldier) and Micaëla perform at center. A singer in the role of Carmen stands to the left (wearing a dark skirt and vest). Scenery flats are painted to represent mountains and trees. Photographed from the balcony, the conductor and musicians in the orchestra pit and audience members in view. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

title_tesim: Filming on Malibou Lake, Malibou Lake Mountain Club, Agoura Hills, 1941
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2939. Malibu [sic] Lake area. 1941.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Santa Monica Mountains National Recreation Area (Calif.)
description_tesim: Photograph of a film or television production shoot on Malibou Lake. Several people watch from the pier as filming takes place. A camera is set up on a mount near the right side of the pier.  Two men stand on either side of the camera. One, wearing a sailor cap, crouches to look through the view finder as two men in a speed boat make their way to the pier where a third man awaits. Canoes with ores are located at foreground and on the opposite side of the pier. An island of eucalyptus trees is viewed beyond the pier at photo center; a small sailboat is viewed center left. Homes are seen nestled in the surrounding Agoura hills at left; the Santa Monica mountains at right.

Malibou Lake Club (renamed Malibou Lake Mountain Club) was developed as a lake-side residential community in 1922. The lake site has been used as a location for films and television shows since the silent movie era. Malibou Lake is situated in the Santa Monica Mountains Recreation Area, Malibu Creek State Park adjacent.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica Mountains National Recreation Area (Calif.)

title_tesim: “Merry Widow” rehearsal performance, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, possibly 1960
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 33
Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.

Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in “The Merry Widow,” Act 1. Natalie Garrotto, portraying Natalie Zeta (Valencienne Popoff) performs at center stage while fellow cast members (portraying party guests) look on. The scene is a ball being held at the fictional Marsovian (Pontevedrian) embassy in Paris. The women wear evening gowns; the men wear tuxedos and military dress uniforms. Decorative set pieces with intricate floral designs flank a faux marble staircase. Visible stage props include chairs and a settee. Above the stage, microphones can be seen hanging from behind a border curtain. Beyond the stage, musicians in less formal attire are viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground. This is a rehearsal performance. A member of the stage crew can be seen at far left wearing street clothing. Also, seating behind the orchestra pit is empty except for one child seen resting against the railing, and one woman wearing a head scarf, seen sitting at far left.
English language adaptations of “The Merry Widow” include several name changes. Here, names from the original Viennese work are noted in parentheses after the variation.

title_tesim: “Merry Widow” production, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, possibly 1960

description_tesim: Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.
English language adaptations of “The Merry Widow” include several name changes. Here, names from the original Viennese work are noted in parentheses after the variation.

Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in “The Merry Widow,” Act 3. Two male opera singers stand slightly forward from a crowd of onlookers as one sings. He wears a tuxedo with a sash across his chest and may be the character, Baron Zeta (Popoff), an Ambassador. He is singing to a performer portraying Prince Danilo (Count Danilo), wearing a military dress uniform and boots. Two men in tuxedos sit at a table on the left. They talk amongst themselves are being served by wait staff. The set, with its cloth covered tables and swag drapery against the stage backdrop, is the decor of Maxim’s - a Parisian nightclub. Beyond the stage, musicians can be seen in the orchestra pit at foreground. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

title_tesim: Oldsmobile Limited automobile with driver, 1939
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2985. Oldsmobile auto made in 1909 cost $5500. Photo 2/2/39. Car stored 25 years "good as new" in 1939.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
ark:/21198/zz002d796n (document)
description_tesim: The car was purchased by C. Standlee Martin (an Oldsmobile dealer based in Long Beach) from its original owner. The car's license plate suggests that it was on display at the Golden Gate Exposition in 1939. For more information on this car, see the article, "Ancient Autos Resurrected: General Motors Hosts to Horseless Carriage Organization," Los Angeles Times, 12 Mar. 1939:E3.

Photograph of a 1909 Oldsmobile Limited being driven on a street. The driver is a man wearing a shirt, jacket, pants and flat cap. He is seated behind a steering wheel that is situated on the right side of the vehicle. The luxury auto has a large wheel base and rides on massive spoke wheels. The tall chassis requires two running boards to climb into the car. A spare tire rests atop the lower running board and is attached to the driver side door with metal bars and straps. All trim, lantern side lights and headlights are brass (standard for this vehicle). License plate frame reads: “C. Stanlee Martin Long Beach.” License plate reads: “California World’s Fair 39 3Y954.” In the background, an electricity tower sits at the edge of a vacant lot, giving way to what looks like a residential neighborhood in the distance. Location unidentified, but possibly Long Beach (where the car’s owner worked and lived).
For related clipping see "Relation" note.

Prior to the Model T, most automobiles had right-side steering wheels even though Americans have always driven on the right side of the road. Henry Ford altered the design, making left-steering standard with mass production of the Model T.

Caption above photograph: 5843. Patio in village of San Juan Capistrano, 1885. Hacienda Aguilar, north wing, showing adobe oven & old Spanish well. The Indian servant is Rosario Cervantes a retainer of the Aguilares. The stout man is Max "Don Marcos" Mendelson whose "Mission Inn" hotel stood just south of the Aguilar Hacienda. A. Yorba.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Six REO Motor cars, each carrying three to five persons, photographed on an unpaved Santa Paula thoroughfare. The men, women and children wear early 1900s era clothing. One man (second car from right) wears a driving cap with goggles.  All six automobiles share a similar engine compartment design and have lantern headlights. Slight variations appear elsewhere (chassis height and width, seating). Only the vehicle at center has a canopy top and front window. Also, note the right side steering wheels seen in each automobile. The cars and their passengers are photographed in front of two Victorian style homes, viewed to the right of a plot of land populated with trees. Picket fences line the road, along with four utility poles in view.

title_tesim: Article, "The 20-Mule Team Rolls Again," 1954
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1896. Death Valley. Old 20-mule team.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Article, "The 20-Mule Team Rolls Again," describes efforts made to recreate the famous mule team for an episode of a television series called, "Death Valley Days." The episode that featured the team was titled, "The Big Team Rolls." The article discusses the history of the mule teams that hauled borax out of Death Valley for the Pacific Coast Borax Company, and describes people like Bruce Morgan who continued to run pack mules and occasionally "hitched up his mules to the old borax wagons for parades and centennials."

Notation at the bottom of the left-hand page reads, "TW 3-7-54," likely providing an abbreviated publication name and date (March 7, 1954). The discussed episode of "Death Valley Days," titled, "The Big Team Rolls," aired January 27, 1955.
Related photograph (see "Relation" note).

title_tesim: Brochure advertising the city of Santa Monica, 1934
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2638 P1
Text from negative sleeve: 2638 P1 to 2. City of Santa Monica, 1934. Folder. Photos of.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Portions of this brochure were used in two mock-up advertisements created by Bartlett. See image ark nos. 21198/zz002d7btf, and 21198/zz002d7brd.

Photograph of a 4-panel publicity brochure describing the city of Santa Monica. The brochure relates the city's notable characteristics using photographs and text. Panel 1 includes an image of Santa Monica Beach with the Breakers Beach Club, Edgewater Beach Club, and Hotel Casa Del Mar, and an image of palm trees atop a Palisades cliff overlooking the bay. Panel 1 text reads: "Santa Monica, California. The City with a Standardized Climate. 64 [degrees] Average Year-round Temperature. Only 20 degrees difference between the High and Low Temperatures for the year..." Panels 2 and 3 are comprised of 8 images with captions (4 per panel). The photos flank a description of Santa Monica's attractions and amenities. Image captions read: " A night beach party on Pacific sands; Society lawn fete in formal Santa Monica garden; Palisades Park Palms above the Pacific; The cool patio of a Santa Monica Beach Club; Typical monster week-end beach crowd; Malibu beach homes of movie stars near Santa Monica; Outdoors in Semi-tropic Santa Monica; "The Pike" Ocean Park Amusement Zone." Center text reads: "You'll Enjoy Every Hour spent in Santa Monica or Ocean Park. Population about 40, 000, in Los Angeles Co., 15 miles from Los Angeles, reached by wide boulevards through the Southland's proudest residential sections. Its beautiful parks include the towering Palisades, with its magnificent palms, 100 feet above the sea. New million dollar breakwater and yacht harbor. Wonderful publicly-owned and police-regulated beaches, with year-round, highly trained municipal life guard service. Surf and still water bathing. The Ocean Park amusement section- famed throughout the nation. Pier and deep-sea fishing - a real fisherman's Paradise that is not excelled in the world. Gorgeous, divinely-hued sunsets. Municipal all-grass golf course, municipal airport, municipal free archery. Municipal tennis courts. Santa Monica is the home of many national and international tennis stars. Twelve country clubs within a few miles. Polo fields nationally known. Outstanding artists' colony; carious civic enterprises sponsored by a high type of citizenship. A public school system which embraces the most modern thought in educational methods. A Junior College with a large enrollment. Will Rogers, Marion Davies, Leo Carrillo and 150 other famous moving picture stars, producers and directors, have homes in Santa Monica and environs. Magnificent beach and swimming clubs, representing investment of millions of dollars. Splendidly appointed hotels and apartments at rates to fit every purse, as well as rentable houses of every size and style. This folder compiled by the City Publicity Commission, City Hall, Santa Monica, Los Angeles County, California." Panel 4 is comprised of a map illustrating points of interest between Downtown Los Angeles and Santa Monica.

description_tesim: The Horseless Carriage Club of Los Angeles is a regional affiliate of the Horseless Carriage Club of America. Its goal is to preserve or restore gas, steam, and electric vehicles manufactured before January 1, 1916. ("The Horseless Carriage Club." The Horseless Carriage Club of America. Accessed December 11, 2014. http://www.hcca.org/hcca.html.)
The Oldsmobile featured in this article was photographed by Bartlett a month prior (see "Relation" note).

Newspaper clipping of an article titled, "Ancient Autos Resurrected: General Motors Hosts to Horseless Carriage Organization." The article regards the Horseless Carriage Club's visit to the General Motors manufacturing plant in South Gate. The accompanying photograph is of two men standing next to a 1909 Oldsmobile Limited (Oldsmobile is a division of General Motors). The man at left wears a suit and tie with hat; the man at right wears a touring coat, driving gloves and cap. Caption under photograph reads: "R. J. Wilkins, left, plant manager of the Southern California division of General Motors, and Arthur P. Twohy, president of the Horseless Carriage Club, inspect the 1909 Oldsmobile Limited belong to C. Standlee Martin of Long Beach." Article published in the Los Angeles Times, 12 Mar. 1939:E3.

Accompanying text reads: "Only 20 degrees difference between the high and Low Temperatures for the year..."
This is a manipulated image copied from a Santa Monica publicity brochure. Bartlett enlarged the original image and cropped the top half. For comparison, see image ark no. 21198/zz002d7bsx.

caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2847. Admission Day 1937. Santa Monica Parade. Bill Hart Jr, Irvin S Cobb, Leo Carrillo.

Native Daughters of the Golden West
Native Sons of the Golden West
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

description_tesim: The parade route began on 4th St. at Colorado Ave., followed north along 4th St. to Wilshire Blvd., and turned westward toward Ocean Ave. where it moved south. The procession passed the review stand at Ocean and Broadway before ending at Pico Blvd.

The marching band of the Benevolent and Protective Order of Elks, Lodge No. 906, participating in the California Admission Day Parade. The band is led by a drum major (at center), followed closely by three flag bearers carrying an American flag, a California flag, and the standard for the Elks Lodge No. 906, which reads, in part: “B.P.O.[E.] No. 906 [Santa] Monica Cal.”  The brass band (wearing military visor caps, jackets and “Sam Browne” belts - wide belts with a narrow strap across the chest) follow. Spectators line the route following 4th Street. Here they are photographed at 4th and Santa Monica Blvd., facing east toward H.S. Henshey’s department store. Signs on adjacent businesses read: “Noonan’s,” “Santa Monica Sporting,” “Southern California Edison,” and “…Business College.”

In 1937, Santa Monica hosted the California Admission Day celebration and convention of the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West. The three-day event (held September 8-10), commemorated the 87th anniversary of California’s admission to the Union. The Los Angeles Times published several articles between September 7th and 10th that described planned festivities, but a more detailed account of the celebration can be found in The Grizzly Bear, a magazine published by the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West (1905-1954). The article is titled, "Happy Memories of Admission Day," The Grizzly Bear, 1 Oct. 1937: 3.

description_tesim: The parade route began on 4th St. at Colorado Ave., followed north along 4th St. to Wilshire Blvd., and turned westward toward Ocean Ave. where it moved south. The procession passed the review stand at Ocean and Broadway before ending at Pico Blvd.
Carrillo is photographed on the second half of the parade route along Ocean Ave. moving south. A second photo shows near the beginning of the parade along 4th St. (image ark no. 21198/zz002d7f94).

Photograph of Leo Carrillo riding a Palomino in the California Admission Day Parade. Carrillo (wearing his characteristic wide-brimmed cowboy hat, with cowboy boots and a gun belt) is photographed next to another man on horseback who looks directly at the camera. The man (possibly a parade official) wears a hat, suit and tie, and riding boots. A badge is pinned to his jacket. The men were photographed on Ocean Ave., probably in the vicinity of Santa Monica Blvd.

He may have been one of several police chiefs who participated in the parade). The men were photographed in the Mayor’s office, Santa Monica City Hall.

title_tesim: Colonel Robert A. Bringham and John F. Eaton, G.A.R. veteran, at the Armistice Day parade, Santa Monica, 1938
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2876. Armistice Day 1938, Santa Monica, also Soldiers Home. Col. RA Bringham Mgr, John F. Eaton, Veteran (age 92) of John A Martin Post GAR. Parade of Santa Monica Streets.

This photograph appears with the caption: "Birthday to Mark End of Civil War Unit. John F. Eaton, right, of John A Martin G.A.R. Post, who will join Thomas Carter, his only surviving comrade, in disbanding the unit next Jan. 25. Col. R. A. Bringham, manager of veterans facility at Sawtelle, is shown with Eaton." [Los Angeles Times, 14 Nov. 1938]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Santa Monica's Armistice Day parade is noted in the article, "Southland Cities Ready for Patriotic Services: Parade, School Pageants and Assemblies to Feature Armistice Observances Today," Los Angeles Times, 11 Nov. 1938.

Photograph of Col. Robert A. (Amedee) Bringham, manager of the Sawtelle Veterans Home (National Home for Disabled Volunteer Soldiers, Pacific Branch) and John F. Eaton, Civil War veteran (representing Atchison / John A. Martin G.A.R. Post 93, Kansas) at the Armistice Day parade in Santa Monica. A flag viewed in the background includes text that reads: "[Vet]erans of Foreign [Wars of the] United States." The group's emblem (a cross of Malta with the organization's name encircling an eagle topped with thirteen stars and a flag at center) is placed above the text. The men were probably photographed on the parade review stand.

title_tesim: Colonel Robert A. Bringham saluting along with two other veterans at the Armistice Day parade, Santa Monica, 1938
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2876. Armistice Day 1938, Santa Monica, also Soldiers Home. Col. RA Bringham Mgr, John F. Eaton, Veteran (age 92) of John A Martin Post GAR. Parade of Santa Monica Streets.

funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Santa Monica's Armistice Day parade is noted in the article, "Southland Cities Ready for Patriotic Services: Parade, School Pageants and Assemblies to Feature Armistice Observances Today," Los Angeles Times, 11 Nov. 1938.

Col. Robert A. (Amedee) Bringham, manager of the Sawtelle Veterans Home (National Home for Disabled Volunteer Soldiers, Pacific Branch), photographed saluting with two other veterans at the Armistice Day parade in Santa Monica. A flag viewed in the background includes text that reads: "Unknown [Soldiers Post 875], West Los [Angeles, California], Mustered in Jan. [3rd]...Veterans of Foreign Wars of United States." The group's emblem (a Cross of Malta with the organization's name encircling an eagle and flag topped with thirteen stars) is placed at center. A second flag reads, in part, "Sawtelle...Soldiers...American" and includes an emblem at center. Additional men can be seen in the background talking and walking about. In the foreground, two microphones are placed in front of what is possibly the parade review stand.

title_tesim: Procession of Army artillery trucks and gunners at the Armistice Day parade, Santa Monica, 1938
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2876. Armistice Day 1938, Santa Monica, also Soldiers Home. Col. RA Bringham Mgr, John F. Eaton, Veteran (age 92) of John A Martin Post GAR. Parade of Santa Monica Streets.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Santa Monica's Armistice Day parade is noted in the article, "Southland Cities Ready for Patriotic Services: Parade, School Pageants and Assemblies to Feature Armistice Observances Today," Los Angeles Times, 11 Nov. 1938.

A procession of Army artillery trucks mounted with air defense artillery and carrying gunners, photographed during the Santa Monica Armistice Day parade. The gunners wear dress uniforms and helmets with badges. The men are part of the California Army National Guard, 115th Quartermaster Regiment. Identifying information is painted on each truck, though only the two truck in the foreground carries legible information. Printed on the hood: "U.S.A. W-38150." [Text surrounding an emblem printed on the driver-side door:] "115 CAL NG D."  The emblem belongs to the Quartermaster Corps' branch. It is comprised of an eagle with wings spread, perched on a wheel set with 13 stars. Superimposed on the wheel are a crossed sword and skeleton key.

description_tesim: Santa Monica's Armistice Day parade is noted in the article, "Southland Cities Ready for Patriotic Services: Parade, School Pageants and Assemblies to Feature Armistice Observances Today," Los Angeles Times, 11 Nov. 1938.

Photograph of Navy officers and a Navy marching band in the Santa Monica Armistice Day parade. The men are led by officers wearing dress uniforms and carrying swords over their right shoulders. They are followed by a line of enlisted men in parade uniform, including the characteristic sailor hat, scarf at neck, and spats (leggings). The sailors are flanked by two flag bearers. The flag at right reads "U.S. Mississippi." A Navy marching band and additional units can be seen bringing up the rear.

title_tesim: Girl holding a basket seated outdoors next to a nest with eggs at the Children's Home Society, Los Angeles, 1935-1960
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2593. Childrens Home. LA. Selected various - Printed stock and magazine sales.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of a little girl seated on grass outdoors holding a basket and an egg with a nest of eggs on the ground beside her. A little house is behind her.

The Children's Home Society of California is a non-profit child welfare agency founded in 1891. It became the first licensed child placement agency in California in 1911. It was located at 919 E. 25th St. from the 1920's up to 1942, when the organization moved to the Ozro W. Childs mansion at 3100 W. Adams Blvd. where it remained until 1977.

title_tesim: Street scene along N. 8th St., Colton, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2852. Redlands, San Bernardino, 1937.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2852
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of N. 8th Street in Colton. Here, a car is viewed passing through an intersection at foreground left, prompted by an Acme traffic signal's "Go" flag (a second signal is stationed catty-corner at left). Three additional traffic signs are viewed behind the signal at foreground: a "Stop" sign, a directional sign, and a U-turn sign. The directional sign uses arrows to guide drivers to west and east-bound cities. It reads, in part: "Ontario...,Los Angeles...,San Bernardino 3 1/2, Redlands 8." A Citizens National Bank building is viewed on the corner at left, with several men standing outside. The next building houses three businesses. Signage reads: "Duke's Liquors;" "Hunter's Sweet Shop, Giant Malt 10...[?], Candies;" Cornet 5 10 25 Cent Store." Next door, a theatre advertises the movie, "Stella Dallas" with Barbara Stanwyck, John Boles, and Anne Shirley. A Union 76 gas station sits at the end of the street. More stores are viewed on the right-side of the street, including a business advertising new and used furniture. This sign is next to a street clock. A truck carrying a cement mixer is seen at the corner with a worker standing behind it, while pedestrians walk across the partially blocked street.
Hunter's Sweet Shop was located on 227 N. 8th St. in Colton, California. Duke's Liquor was located on 225 N. 8th St., north of Citizen's Bank, as noted in the San Bernardino County Sun, 3 Mar. 1938.

title_tesim: Street scene along Orange St. from the vicinity of Redlands Boulevard, Redlands, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2852. Redlands, San Bernardino, 1937.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2852
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of Orange Street, looking toward the E. State Street intersection, photographed from the vicinity of Redlands Boulevard. Businesses and 1930s era automobiles line the paved streets, while power lines and a radio tower transverse across and loom above buildings. At foreground left, a drug store stands next to a Bank of America (built 1919, demolished 1959). Across State Street on the left stands a building that once housed Star Grocery. Here, signage along the ground floor reads: "...Market," "50...-Lunch," "Toilet Articles," "Annibil's Drugs, Fountain Service." A vertical sign reading "Dentist" hangs from the second floor. Further down the street, another vertical sign reading "Edison" is viewed hanging from a three-story building. A Security First National Bank stands catty-corner from Bank of America (at right).

Photograph of the San Bernardino County Courthouse at 351 North Arrowhead Avenue with pine trees and palm trees planted on the lawn in front. A broadcasting tower is viewed behind the Classical Revival building. Banning House, or the General Phineas Banning Residence Museum, is located in the Wilmington neighborhood of Los Angeles. The home (built in 1863) includes a garden with eucalyptus trees and large wisteria vines.

description_tesim: The Women's Ambulance and Defense Corps was a volunteer organization open to women ages 18-45. Along with Red Cross First Aid courses, the women were trained in a litany of subjects including: ambulance (and heavy vehicle) driving and repair, mechanics, X-ray, map reading, semaphoring, radio sending and receiving, and photography.

Photograph of a Women's Ambulance Defense Corps unit, in uniform, standing at attention on the steps of Santa Monica City Hall. A banner with the American colors and eagle reading, "E. Pluribus Unum," hangs above the entrance, flanked by two stripped banners. Below, American flags are held by six Corps members in the back row.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica City Hall (Santa Monica, Calif.)

description_tesim: Photograph of Walter E. Scott (known as "Death Valley Scotty") and Albert Mussey Johnson riding in a car during the "Roundup of Stars" parade - part of the Will Rogers Memorial Celebration.

Related to Los Angeles Times article, “Tribute Paid Will Rogers: Five Thousand Persons and 1400 Horses March in Santa Monica Parade Santa Monica Honors Rogers Hundred Thousand See Humorist's Favorite Horse in Parade,” 28 Jul. 1940: A1.

The Will Rogers Memorial Celebration and Pageant was a three-day event with proceeds going to the Red Cross. In 1940, the event coincided with the national convention of the Will Rogers Memorial Highway (U.S. 66) Association. Festivities included a tour of Will Rogers Ranch for convention delegates, a rodeo held at Municipal Stadium, and a nightly pageant held at Santa Monica High School.

title_tesim: Altar at Church of Epiphany, Los Angeles, 1941
caption_tesim: Text on negative sleeve: No. 3021, Church of Epiphany, 1941 (circled), Sichel and Altura St., Los Angeles, altar Cross of, Arthur Merick Byam, 1896, For Light & Life Press, Winona Lake, Indiana
named_subject_tesim: Church of the Epiphany (Los Angeles, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This is an image of the altar at the Church of Epiphany in Los Angeles. On top of the altar are 2 candles in candle holders, framed text and a book with 3 book marks. On a stand above the altar is the cross that was dedicated to Arthur Merick Byam, flanked by 6 candles in candle holders. The Cross rests on a stand with the inscription reading "This do in Remembrance of Me." In a triple arcade across the front of the altar is a relief of a Lamb of God with a cruciform halo and holding a banner with a cross. The Lamb is flanked by the Alpha and Omega. A wooden throne is partially visible on on the left.
combined_subject_ssim: Church of the Epiphany (Los Angeles, Calif.)

description_tesim: This is an image of the silver (?) altar cross at the Church of Epiphany in Los Angeles. It is decorated with a relief pattern of flowers and leaves, with the letters 'LHC' intertwined at the intersection. The cross was donated in memory of Arthur Merick Byam (1896). It  cross stands on a three-tiered base, with each tier inscribed individually. The topmost tier reads, "To the Glory of GOD and in loving memory of," the middle tier reads, "Arthur Merick Byam, Aged 55." and the base tier quotes, "But God is good."
combined_subject_ssim: Church of the Epiphany (Los Angeles, Calif.)

title_tesim: Portrait painting of a horse by E. Nunn Miller, Palm Springs, 1942
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2761. Palm Springs. Betty Shafer. 3/6/42. Miller Horse.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Evylena Nunn (E. Nunn) Miller moved from Kansas to Santa Ana in 1903 and earned her BA from Pomona College. In southwest she painted landscapes, coastal views and Native Americans, traveling widely to Arizona, New Mexico, Utah, and Colorado. She painted 40 canvases of scenes of life in the pueblos of the Navajo, Apache, Toas, Hopi, Jemez, Acoma, Laguna, and Zuni. One of her goals was to paint all of the pueblos of the Southwest. Her 1933 book "Travel Tree" was about her journey to achieve this goal.
Portrait of a horse displayed in a Palm Springs location, signed "E. Nunn Miller" and dated 1942.

title_tesim: Cowboy musicians and guests at Rogers Ranch resort, Palm Springs, 1941

description_tesim: Three cowboy musicians, 5 guests and 2 horses seated on and standing around a platform at Rogers Ranch resort. The  musicians  play a guitar, ukulele and bass.

Clayton A. Dills won a seat in the California State Assembly in 1942 and, with his brother Ralph C. Dills, became the first team of brothers to ever serve in the state Assembly. Ralph also became a State Senator. Clayton and Ralph, and their brothers Paris and Curly, were also musicians who performed together.

title_tesim: Still life set-up with flowers, perhaps for an art class at Santa Monica High School, Santa Monica, 1937-1939
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: SMHi Art Rm. Open House Nite. [Sleeve is an envelope addressed to Mr. Adelbert Bartlett with postal cancelation: "Los Angeles, Calif., Apr 25, 1937."]
