description_tesim: Photographs housed with this negative sleeve are related to others from a sleeve labeled "Carolyn's negatives." The photographs are visually and thematically similar. In addition, both sleeves contained photographs of Santa Monica High School structures built between 1937 and 1939 by the Works Project Administration (WPA). Both sleeves also included construction site images, likely related to the WPA’s reconstruction project (image ark nos. 21198/zz002d8j8h and 198/zz002d8k9h).

Still life set-up, perhaps for an art class instruction, is comprised of flowers placed in a shallow ceramic or porcelain bowl, with a decanter placed slightly behind it. A two-panel folding screen acts as a background for the still life. A blurred man on the left who moved during the exposure is wearing a button down shirt and pants. Photographed at Santa Monica High School, probably by Carolyn Bartlett.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica High School (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: "Wynken, Blynken & Nod" float at the Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade, Santa Monica, 1936
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2927, Ocean Park, Children's Parade, 1936
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Also see image ark no. 21198/zz002d8np5, for image of Leonie Lester Show Boat.
The event was covered in a Los Angeles Times article, "Hundreds of Children to be in Floral Parade," 27 Aug. 1936: 10.

"Wynken, Blynken & Nod" float at the Seventeenth Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade. Three children dressed identically in white hats and coats hold up a sign reading, "Wynken, Blynken & Nod". The sign has a badge numbered "109" tied to the top. A girl, participant number 149,  precedes the float. In the background another float, "Leonie Lest[er] Show Bo[at]" follows.

The Ocean Park Annual Children's Floral Parade was a community event started in 1919. The 1936 parade was organized by the Santa Monica clubwomen and civic leaders with Mayor Gillette and the city council also lending their support. Flowers were provided by city parks and private gardens.

The Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade was a community event started in 1919. The 1936 parade was organized by the Santa Monica clubwomen and civic leaders, with Mayor Gillette and the city council also lending their support. Flowers were provided by city parks and private gardens.

Photograph of a "Fancy Dress" entrant at the Seventeenth Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade. The young girl, dressed in a bright frock, carries a basket of Posies labelled, "Posies, 5c." She is also wearing a floral hat and holding up a parasol covered in flowers. A badge reading "174" is tied around her right upper arm.

description_tesim: The negative sleeve references the  California State Elks Association, held September 1951. Santa Monica served as host city. For photograph of Jean Moorhead used in this composite, see image ark no. 21198/zz002d8tmj.
Barbara Jean Moorhead was an actress and model. She was Playboy Playmate of the Month, October 1955 (under the name Jean Moorehead).

Photomontage image of Jean Moorhead riding the waves on a Santa Monica beach. A cutout of Moorhead, wearing a stripped bathing suit, posing as if riding a surfboard (actually an ironing board) has been superimposed onto a photograph of a Santa Monica beach complete with other surfers, swimmers and sunbathers. Prominently displayed in the background are the Breakers Beach Club, Edgewater Beach Club, and Hotel Casa Del Mar.
combined_subject_ssim: Hotel Casa del Mar (Santa Monica, Calif.)

description_tesim: The Hotel Playa Ensenada opened on October 31, 1930. Later known as the Hotel Riviera del Pacifico, it was an elegant gambling casino developed by the North American Compañía Mexicana del Rosarito. The architect was Gordon E. Mayer. The hotel was closed and partly demolished in 1964. Partly rebuilt in 1978, the hotel was reopened as the Centro Social, Cívico y Cultural de Ensenada

title_tesim: Typewritten description of photograph[s?] of Captain Arthur Tennyson at abandoned plaster workshop
caption_tesim: Text reads: Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, California.  Exclusive.  Captain Arthur Tennyson, upstanding young Englishman from London, late of the King's Coldstream Guards on China duty, elder (by 25 minutes) of the famous Tennyson twins (Arthur and Walter), descendants of the poet Tennyson, visits a "museum" of "ancient" statuary, the same being the abandoned workshop of a plaster roadside statue maker, in Los Angeles.  Capt. Tennyson who is on a visit to this country marvelled at the "extraordinary" number of duplicates of the same dancing couples executed in plaster which were intended for use about the countryside advertising something or other.  Clarice Barclay is the girl in the views.  Arthur Tennyson's twin brother, Walter, who formerly lived in California, now resides in London.  Owing to the striking similarity of the two brothers, they find it embarassing to be seen on the streets together.  And even when they travel about separately they are always mistaken one for the other.  For instance, when Arthur, depicted here, reached Los Angeles he was immediately recognized as his twin brother Walter, who formerly lived here, and in two or three cases was surrounded by admiring young ladies who spoke endearing words, hung on to his hands and even planted a kiss or two upon his fair blonde English cheeks, much to his chagrin.  "But, I'm sure, I shall be able to bear it all in time," says Arthur.
Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2382, 12/1/29, Tennyson, Walter, 7014 Fountain Ave, Statues, Hollywood, Tony Burke.

title_tesim: Mrs. Tom Carlisle seated on rock formation, Riverside County, [1920-1939?]
caption_tesim: Handwritten at edge of negative: 1986

Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 1986 - 1987 - 1988 - 1989 Pls ret negs.  Hemet - Idylwild.  San Jacinto Mountain district, Southern California wonders - Famous pitcher or lily rock, with Henry Barnbrock in view, and kneeling dromedary, a sandstone formation with Mrs. Tom Carlisle "riding."  These formations have been carved by wind and elements.  Discovered and photographed here for first time.  The "dromedary" is complete even to nose and ears.  Camel & lilly rock, cave at Ramona's Burial Place

title_tesim: Typewritten description of photograph of James E. Erwin, San Bernardino County, 1929
caption_tesim: Text reads in part: Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th St, Santa Monica, Cal.  James E. Erwin, known locally as "Jim," at Pine Knot P.O. on Big Bear Lake, in the mountains of San Bernardino County of Southern California, is 78 years old and has lived 45 years in the region ... Jim has been miner, trapper, soldier, moving picture extra. ... moving picture companies ... always enlist the services of Jim Erwin in Klondike scenes ... widely known as a story teller ... known locally as another Trader Horn. ... In the picture he is shown wearing an old Stetson hat which has served him for many seasons on his ranch near Pine Knot.

title_tesim: Montage of Thelma Peairs, Miss Venice, above crowded Venice beach, Venice, 1928
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2211 - 2212 - 2213 - 2214 2215 - 2216 -2217 - 2218 Miss Thelma Peairs, Also with large crowd, 1928.  Miss Thelma Peairs, the official Miss Venice-of-America, popular Los Angeles beach resort, the West's Coney Island, has turned photographer and is always on hand to snap notables visiting the resort.  Between photographing people, attending High School and posing as an artist's model as well as taking an occasional flyer into the movies as a dancer and singer she is a very busy young lady indeed. title_tesim: Coastal view towards road construction on a slope in the Rancho Malibu la Costa development, Malibu, circa 1927
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: View facing south-east towards road construction on slope in the Rancho Malibu la Costa development, with the beach and three beach houses visible along the Pacific Coast Highway beyond. Two plots of land for house construction are on the slope side of the highway. A sign one plot advertises the Rancho Malibu la Costa development. Further down the highway towards the left is Las Flores Canyon Road.

Rancho Topanga Malibu Sequit was a 13,316-acre Spanish land grant given by Spanish Governor José Joaquín de Arrillaga in 1804 to José Bartolomé Tapia. In 1892 Frederick Hastings Rindge and May Knight Rindge, purchased the rancho. In 1926, the Marblehead Land Company, formed by May Knight Rindge, made a $6 million deal with Harold G. Ferguson to develop the La Costa area (between Las Flores Canyon and Carbon Canyon), but that deal fell apart when Ferguson was sentenced to jail for securities fraud in 1931. Ferguson laid out the development with street names as they remain today: Rambla Vista, Paseo Serra, etc. It took until the mid 1930's for development of the area to begin in earnest.

title_tesim: Grace Healy with snow bear, Mount Baldy, 1928
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2378. Camp Baldy. 1928. Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif. A mile in elevation above some of Southern California's flower gardens and orange groves are snowfields where in negatives enclosed co-eds on a late November week-end built snow men--"just had to get a man somehow"--and modelled a very expressive "snow bear" in honor of the California bear, official state "animule." One view shows Grace Healy offering the bear a cup of snow

title_tesim: Vacationers on sunny terrace overlooking the coast of Southern California and fleet of ships, Long Beach 1933
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve:  Long Beach, fleet from roof garden. Compo..., All Year Club, Red Island RR. 1933.

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Probably photographed at the Breakers Hotel (210 E. Ocean Boulevard), that stood adjacent to the Capitol/Tracy Theatre (demolished 1974), across from the Long Beach Municipal Auditorium (now the Long Beach Convention and Entertainment Center).
This image was used in an advertisement for the All Year Club of Southern California, a Los Angeles based travel agency that promoted low cost vacations in Southern California.

Photograph of two women seated at a table on a terrace, with a girl standing apart from them at the terrace wall. A sign for the Capitol Theatre is viewed in the immediate background at left. At center ground, the Long Beach Municipal Auditorium (demolished in 1975) with a fleet of six ships in the ocean beyond.

description_tesim: Robert College is a school located on a sixty-five acre wooded campus on the European side of Istanbul between the two bridges on the Bosphorus. It was founded in 1863 by Americans, Christopher Rhinelander Robert and Cyrus Hamlin. In 1972 the higher education division was transferred to the Turkish government and has continued as Boğaziçi University, Turkey's leading public university.
View towards the Boğaziçi Üniversitesi campus (formerly part of Robert College), and the towers of the Rumeli Hisari (fortress) beyond, facing south across the Bosphorus.

title_tesim: View down slope towards beach houses in the Rancho Malibu la Costa development, Malibu, circa 1927
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: View facing south-east towards road and house construction site in the Rancho Malibu la Costa development, with the beach and beach houses visible along the Pacific Coast Highway beyond. The location is west of the intersection of the Pacific Coast Highway and Las Flores Canyon Road.

Rancho Topanga Malibu Sequit was a 13,316-acre Spanish land grant given by Spanish Governor José Joaquín de Arrillaga in 1804 to José Bartolomé Tapia. In 1892 Frederick Hastings Rindge and May Knight Rindge, purchased the rancho. In 1926, the Marblehead Land Company, formed by May Knight Rindge, made a $6 million deal with Harold G. Ferguson to develop the La Costa area (between Las Flores Canyon and Carbon Canyon), but that deal fell apart when Ferguson was sentenced to jail for securities fraud in 1931. Ferguson laid out the development with street names as they remain today: Rambla Vista, Paseo Serra, etc. It took until the mid 1930's for development of the area to begin in earnest.

description_tesim: The Hotel Playa Ensenada opened on October 31, 1930. Later known as the Hotel Riviera del Pacifico, it was an elegant gambling casino developed by the North American Compañía Mexicana del Rosarito. The architect was Gordon E. Mayer. The hotel was closed and partly demolished in 1964. Partly rebuilt in 1978, the hotel was reopened as the Centro Social, Cívico y Cultural de Ensenada.
Artist Alfredo Ramos Martinez, wearing cardigan sweater and tie, holding palette, at Hotel Playa de Ensenada

From the Alfredo Ramos Martinez Research Project web site: "in 1929, the boxer Jack Dempsey commissioned him to paint murals, create a series of paintings and decorate a chapel at the Hotel-Casino Playa Ensenada (Ensenada, Baja California)."

title_tesim: View towards three beach houses in the Rancho Malibu la Costa development, Malibu, circa 1927
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: View facing south-east from slope with dirt road towards four houses on the ocean side of the Pacific Coast Highway in the Rancho Malibu la Costa development on the Malibu coast. A billboard is visible on the beach. The last building visible in the far distance on the left is the Las Flores Inn at the intersection of the Pacific Coast Highway and Las Flores Canyon Road.

Rancho Topanga Malibu Sequit was a 13,316-acre Spanish land grant given by Spanish Governor José Joaquín de Arrillaga in 1804 to José Bartolomé Tapia. In 1892 Frederick Hastings Rindge and May Knight Rindge, purchased the rancho. In 1926, the Marblehead Land Company, formed by May Knight Rindge, made a $6 million deal with Harold G. Ferguson to develop the La Costa area (between Las Flores Canyon and Carbon Canyon), but that deal fell apart when Ferguson was sentenced to jail for securities fraud in 1931. Ferguson laid out the development with street names as they remain today: Rambla Vista, Paseo Serra, etc. It took until the mid 1930's for development of the area to begin in earnest.

description_tesim: Handwritten letter from Claude Mawby to Adelbert Bartlett, dated Aug 2, 1929, asking if Bartlett can cover the children's birthday party on Saturday, Aug 10th, and listing the children invited.

Text reads, in part: Dear Mr. Bartlett, Did you get my recent long letter to you ... Please let me know without fail if you can cover the children's birthday party Saturday next Aug 10th. We are giving this year on Mr. George Lewis' patio ... I have every thing to see to this year as my poor wife is still ill ... the children of the following are coming to the party ... Yours in great haste, Claude Mawby

title_tesim: Caption for photograph of St. Louis Estes IV, between 1928 and 1936
creator_tesim: Adelbert and Clara Bartlett residence (Santa Monica, Calif.)
caption_tesim: Typewritten caption for photograph reads: St. Louis Estes, the Fourth, one year old [struck out in pencil: yesterday], third back to nature son of the New York publisher, health scientist and lecturer, now a resident of Hollywood, poses for his picture before eating his first birthday cake which was made of uncooked California foods, figs, dates, oranges and nuts.
Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2385. 26 negs. Dr Estes children. Dr St Louis Estes. 1928 to 1936. Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, Los Angeles, California.
Handwritten above top of text: 2385
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Dr. St. Louis Albert Estes (1876-1951) was an American doctor and the author of Raw Food and Health. He lived in Van Nuys, California in the late 1920s and early 1930s, and had 3 children with his first wife in the 1910s, 12 children with his second wife in the 1920s and 1930s.
Text of caption only, no photograph

The National Home for Disabled Volunteer Soldiers (NHDVS), Pacific Branch, was an earlier name, in part, for the United States Department of Veterans Affairs. Photographed on the grounds of what was later named the Greater Los Angeles Veterans Affairs Healthcare System (U.S.)

caption_tesim: Handwritten at edge of negative: 2078

Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2077 2078 - 2079 - 2080 [other numbers torn off at top] Pasadena Rose Tournament NYear 1927 to 1950, 11, 6x8 [Struck out: 1926, 192[4?] 2081 2082 2083 Panorama of Santa Monica bay from Palisades Park, showing beach clubs popular with movie and tourist groups]

description_tesim: "La Paloma" float with white doves suspended over a garden inhabited by 6 persons in Spanish attire, including a man in a hat playing lute near center, and singers Jose Arias, Thea La Forte, Sybil Tyler and Ella Archer. The Pasadena Memorial Flagpole (Goodhue Flagpole) is visible behind the float in its original location in the middle of the intersection of Orange Grove and Colorado Boulevard.
1927 was the first year the the Tournament of Roses Parade had a theme, "Songs in Flowers," with each float typifying a song

Image is of the clipper ship, Tweed, formerly named the Punjaub. The Punjaub was a sail/paddle steamer frigate built by the Indian navy. It was later purchased by Jock Willis, who changed her name to the Tweed and converted her to solely sail propulsion.

architect_tesim: John C. Lindsay, A.I.A., and Associates, Architects and Engineers
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: "6/29/57 [?], John Lindsay, Calif Bank, 6/29/57 [?]".

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This building was designed by John Lindsay, as made evident by one of the signs on the building. John Lindsay was a southern California architect. Lindsay was associated with at least two the firms: Palmer, Krisel & Lindsay, A.I.A. for a brief time around 1952; and John C. Lindsay and Associates.

This image is of the California Bank Brentwood office under construction, which was designed by architect John Lindsay.  Numerous signs can be seen on the front of the building, including one listing the address as "11979 San Vincente Blvd".  However, the address of the building appears to have changed and is now "11950 San Vincente Blvd". Other signs read: "California Bank, Brentwood Office Opening Soon," "Temporary Location 11944 San Vincente, 2 Blocks West," "Wohl-Calhoun Co., General Contractors, 845 N. Highland Ave, Los Angeles HQ4-4188," "For Lease, Offices...in this Bank Bldg. 2nd Floor...Ample Parking...David Barry and Co."

title_tesim: Article about poet Ina Donna Coolbrith with an 1880's portrait and an 1888 shell wreath, Santa Monica, 1953
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve reads: H 2957 File A, Ina Coolbrith, copied 4/30/40, and shell wreath she made in 1880's--Santa Monica, Poet Laureate of California 1914, John Crum
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Ornate 1888 horse-shoe-shaped shell art wreath made by poet Ina Donna Coolbrith in upper left corner. In upper right corner is a  portrait of Ina Donna Coolbrith which is the frontispiece of her book Songs from the Golden Gate, 1895. The lower portion of the page is an article discussing In Coolbrith making the wreath ["Heirloom Shows Poetess' Art," Los Angeles Times, October 12, 1953]. At the time of this photograph the wreath belonged to the Perrys' nephew John H. Crum of Santa Monica. The shell wreath is an example of Victorian era domestic crafts.

title_tesim: Horseshoe-shaped shell art wreath made in 1888 by poet Ina Donna Coolbrith, Santa Monica, 1940
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: H 2957, File A, Ina Coolbrith, copied 4/30/40, and shell wreath she made in 1880's - Santa Monica.  Poet Laureate of California, 1914, John Crum
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Horseshoe-shaped shell art wreath made in 1888 by poet Ina Donna Coolbrith. The wreath was composed of sea shells interwoven with moss gathered at Santa Monica. The flowers represented include Mariposa lily, Shasta daisy, Copa de Oro, camelia, rose and other flowers. The wreath was a gift to Elizabeth Dalton Perry and William Hayes Perry. At the time of this photograph the wreath belonged to the Perrys' nephew John H. Crum of Santa Monica. The shell wreath is an example of Victorian era domestic crafts.

description_tesim: Written on the side of the photo is: 48 Moss.

Mezzo soprano June Moss stands wearing a dirndl, necklace, bracelet, and earrings. She wore this costume while performing in "Martha" at the John Adams Auditorium in Santa Monica. She holds a book in her left hand, resting it on her hip. Curtains are visible behind her.
June Moss was a mezzo soprano who performed with the Santa Monica Civic Opera in the 1950's.

title_tesim: Scouts in attendance at the dedication of Camp Josepho, Pacific Palisades, 1941
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2910, Camp Josepho, Boy Scouts, 6/7/41, Leo Carrillo, Dedication.
named_subject_tesim: Camp Josepho (Pacific Palisades, Los Angeles, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of Boy Scouts at the dedication of Camp Josepho, a Boy Scouting Camp in the Pacific Palisades. They may possibly be listening to Leo Carrillo or another speaker in attendance. A scout musical band can be viewed at foreground. Beyond them, a vast number of scouts populate the campgrounds. The scouts are surrounded by adults (standing at the outer edges of the crowd). Tents are set up at points within the crowd. Cars can be seen parked along a tree-lined road leading to the grounds.
Related to the Los Angeles Times article, "Scouts Receive Camp as Gift: Donors Watch Boys of Crescent Bay Council Enjoy New Facilities," 8 Jun. 1941:18.

title_tesim: Page of article, “Beirut to Bagdad,” circa 1928
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1928. Beirut to Bagdad. Bartlett story. Touring Topics. Photocopy pages. 5.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of the first page of Adelbert Bartlett’s article, “Beirut to Bagdad: A Southern Californian motors through the land of the Arabian Nights.” The page describes the beginnings of a 650 mile trek through the Arabian Desert that followed an automobile mail stage route. Image at top right is of the Temple of the Sun ruins at Baalbek (Temple of Jupiter), Syria. The bottom left image is of an automobile parked on a street in an unidentified city. The car is packed with people and baggage. Presumably one of the transport men (wearing a fez hat) stands outside of the car. Others in similar garb are gathered with Anglos on the opposite side. One appears to wear a pith helmet. Writing on the car hood list cities along the mail route.

In 1928, Adelbert Bartlett wrote and contributed photographs to an article documenting his trip through Lebanon, Syria and Iraq. The work was published as, “Beirut to Bagdad,” Touring Topics, 1928: 32-35, 60. The magazine, started in 1909 (now called Westways) was a publication for members of the Automobile Club of Southern California.

title_tesim: Typewritten narrative about Los Angeles Library murals and Dean Cornwell (pg. 1), 1932
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2334 p 1 to 14, 1932, Los Angeles Library, Dean Cornwell, Artist, 206 E. 33rd St NY city, Los Angeles Library.

Text: Caption for Photograph of Dean Cornwell. Dean Cornwell =, winner of the $50,000 contract for murals for The Los Angeles Public Library. Caught by his friend, the camera-man, Adelbert Bartlett, as he sketched in the Rotunda of the Library, the great room in which his murals are to be placed. photographer and artist met here for the first time in two years and renewed the friendship begun in Palestine where Cornwell's famous paintings of the streets of Jerusalem were made. Dean Cornwell, at the age of thirty-five, finds himself with the enviable title of the highest paid illustrator in the world, and the award of the mural contract for the Los Angeles Public Library one of the great modern public buildings, the last and crowning conception of the architect, Bertram Grosvenor Goodhue. Mr. Cornwell's contract with the Hearst Corporation for exclusive rights to his illustrative work for magazines and books, marks him as the highest paid illustrator in America, and hence in the world. His vignettes in the Cosmopolitan Magazine have established a school of illustration which has been widely imitated. The acceptance of the Los Angeles contract involves sacrifice of much time and temporary postponement of the "highest salary", but the new venture is characteristic of the career of this young American. Born in Louisville, Kentucky, in 1892, young Dean went to Chicago at the age of nineteen, became connected with the art section of the Chicago Tribune, and studied at the Art Institute. On the strength of a single order of illustration of the Red Book, he went to New Work, the mecca of artists. Many a story could be told of his struggles for a foothold. His opportunity finally cam with [...]

description_tesim: This narrative, most likely written by Bartlett, contains information on the career of Cornwell, from his early education to the time the narrative was written in 1932.

This article is about Dean Cornwell, an American painter and illustrator who was prominent in the early 20th century. His works include illustrations for Harper's Bazaar, Cosmopolitan, and Good Housekeeping. He also painted several other murals besides those for the L.A. library, including for the locations: the Warwick Hotel New York, 10 Rockefeller Plaza New York, and the Sevier State Office Building in Tennessee. He is mentioned in several L.A. Times articles, including one dated June 21, 1931, which covers his research process for the murals for the L.A. library.

description_tesim: This image is of Monty (Plummer H.) Montgomery, a lapidarist who worked and lived in Santa Monica. To his right are several cases of stone specimens carved into various shapes such as: hearts, stars, and ovals.

subject_tesim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)
description_tesim: At some point after WWII the rustic branch fence at Palisades park was replaced with two types: a pre-cast concrete fence with a criss-cross design and a wood board fence. Bartlett photographs of Palisades Park dated 1946 show the rustic branch fence and the wood rail fence.

Photograph of two palm trees atop the Palisades Cliffs. Beyond the trees at left, a portion of the cliff face and rustic eucalyptus branch fence can be viewed. View looks south toward a stretch of Pacific Coast Highway (California Highway 1), with cars lining the road and parked in lots near what are presumably beach homes, clubs and businesses. These structures follow along the Highway and beach- the beach populated by crowds on the sand and in the water. At center sits the Santa Monica Pier and Yacht Harbor, partially obscured by the palm tree at right.  Beyond the pier, the coastline continues into the distance. Photographed at the foot of Montana Avenue, above the Sorrento Beach Club.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)

Handwritten on edge of negative: 2889 P 3

subject_tesim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)
description_tesim: At some point after WWII the rustic branch fence at Palisades park was replaced with two types: a pre-cast concrete fence with a criss-cross design and a wood board fence. Bartlett photographs of Palisades Park dated 1946 show the rustic branch fence and the wood rail fence.

Photograph of Carolyn Bartlett seated, reading, beneath one of two palm trees on the Palisades Park cliffs. A portion of the cliffs and wooden rail fence can be seen at left, with structures along Pacific Coast Highway (California Highway 1) and the Santa Monica coastline in view at right. Photographed at the foot of Montana Avenue, above the Sorrento Beach Club.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Santa Monica coastline from the Palisades Park cliffs at Montana Avenue, Santa Monica, 1946-1952
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2889 P 7
Text from negative sleeve: 2889 P1 to 8. Santa Monica. 2 Palms – foot of Montana Ave. above Sorrento Club. 1946, 1952. Miss Carolyn Bartlett on view. Show file #.

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)
description_tesim: Photograph of the Santa Monica coastline from the Palisades Park cliffs, looking south. At foreground, a large palm tree and rock cliffs frame the image below. The view faces south toward a stretch of Pacific Coast Highway (California Highway 1), with cars lining the road and parked in lots near what are presumably beach homes, clubs and businesses. These structures follow along the Highway and beach- the beach populated by crowds on the sand and in the water. At center sits the Santa Monica Pier and Yacht Harbor. Beyond the pier, the coastline continues into the distance. Photographed at the foot of Montana Avenue, above the Sorrento Beach Club.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: View of the Pacific Ocean from a residence, Huntington Palisades, Pacific Palisades, 1940-1946
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2890 P 4 to 9. Huntington Palisades, Pacific Palisades, Santa Monica area above Will Rogers State Beach Lighthouse. See 2890 P 1 for best negative.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph is one of a succession of photos labeled 2890 P 4-9. The estimated creation date for photographs labeled P 4 and P 5 is 1940-1946, based on the date of a third image taken at the same location (see image ark nos. 21198/zz002d4xkp, 21198/zz002d4xm6, and 21198/zz002d86c7).

View of the Pacific Ocean from a residence seen at left. The brick structure is surrounded by trees and shrubbery. A eucalyptus tree stands at right. The property was probably located on the bluffs above Santa Monica Canyon, in the Huntington Palisades neighborhood.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2890 P 8

Huntington Palisades is a neighborhood situated on the bluffs north of Santa Monica Canyon. The land was first purchased by Abbott Kinney in 1887. Kinney planted eucalyptus trees with the intention of using the wood for railroad ties. When that proved to be untenable, he sold the entire property. Subsequent landowners, including Collis Huntington and Robert C. Gillis, further developed the land into residential lots. The development was eventually completed by Mark Daniels and W.W. Williams, with the first home built on the property in 1926.

title_tesim: "Rigoletto" production, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1949
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 125
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Santa Monica Civic Opera performed “Rigoletto” at the John Adams Auditorium November 26-27, and December 3-4.

Scene in a Santa Monica Civic Opera production of "Rigoletto." Rigoletto at center stage (Enrico Porta) kneels with hands on head as Count Monterone (R, Laurin Malcom) and the Duke of Mantua (L, Raymond Gagan) sing and a crowd of nobles and courtiers watch the events transpire, including Natalie Garrotto (2nd from left) and Jean Kortlander (3rd from R). The singers wear costumes evoking 16th century attire. The female singers wear long gowns with full skirts and long, full sleeves. The men wear stockings, doublet-inspired tunics, ruff collars, and baldrics (for holding a sword) around the waist. The stage set represents a room in the Duke’s palace complete with a throne (an ornate wooden chair covered with a canopy, placed on an elevated platform).  Musicians are in the orchestra pit in the  foreground.

title_tesim: "Rigoletto" production with Enrico Porta and Diana Guadagno (possibly), John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1949
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 131
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Santa Monica Civic Opera performed “Rigoletto” at the John Adams Auditorium November 26-27, and December 3-4. This is probably the November 27th performance, with Diana Guadagno in the role of Gilda. The role alternated between Natalie Garrotto, Kay Marshall and Diana Guadagno. Enrico Porta played Rigoletto for all four shows.

Opera singers performing in “Rigoletto.” The female singer, likely Diana Guadagno in the role of Gilda, wears a long satin gown with gigot sleeves and ballet flats. Enrico Porta, in the role of Rigoletto, wears a doublet-inspired stripped tunic, a small ruff collar, full, short breeches (called slops), and stockings. They stand near a bench within a walled garden. Background flats are painted to mimic stone walls and a fountain. One wall has a functional open door. Musicians can be seen in the orchestra pit at foreground.

description_tesim: A notice for this production, conducted by Gaylord Browne and performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 17 Nov. 1957: F8

Poster advertising a Santa Monica Civic Opera production of "Hansel and Gretel." Provides performance dates and times, and list the conductor, stage producer, and starring cast members. Sheet music for the “Children’s Prayer” accompanied by a photograph of a praying child attached to the poster. Production presented at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
It appears that Bartlett manipulated this poster to create a collage using articles and photographs related to this performance. (see "Relation" note).

description_tesim: Gladys Andrews as as Maddalena and Ray Gagan as the Duke of Mantua in a Santa Monica Civic Opera production of "Rigoletto." Andrews wears a Romani (Gypsy) style outfit with full skirt embroidered at the hem, an off-the-shoulder peasant blouse, sandals, beaded necklaces, bracelets, and a flower behind one ear. Gagan wears stockings and a doublet-inspired tunic with a small ruff at the neck and cuffs. The stage set is the interior of a dwelling with a fireplace, stucco walls, curtained windows and a door. A cloth-covered table and chair complete the scenery.
Image reversed.

title_tesim: "Pagliacci" production, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1955
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 22
Text from the negative sleeve: Barber. June Moss. #10–11–12–15–16–17-18. Barber of Seville. Dec 1955. [Negative misfiled].
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Pagliacci, a drama in two acts, was performed in a double-bill with Pietro Mascagni’s opera, Cavalleria Rustica, a melodrama in one act.

Opera singers performing in Ruggero Leoncavallo’s, Pagliacci. Canio (dressed as Pagliaccio in a black and white satin clown costume and cap) stands to the left of Beppe (dressed as Arlecchino or Harlequin) who handles a drum. Cast members dressed as Calabrian villagers take up the stage space. The painted backdrop and scenery depict a country village complete with rolling hills and trees, as well as a brick dwelling (possibly a tavern). The set is completed by a temporary stage set up for Pagliaccio’s troupe. This secondary stage resembles a Greek temple and is positioned downstage left, its interior obscured by curtains. Musicians are viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground. Performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
A notice for this production, conducted by Mario Lanza and performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 7 Aug. 1955: E7

description_tesim: Pagliacci, a drama in two acts, was performed in a double-bill with Pietro Mascagni’s opera, Cavalleria Rustica, a melodrama in one act.
A notice for this production, conducted by Mario Lanza and performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 7 Aug. 1955: E7.

Opera singers portraying Nedda and Silvio, in Ruggero Leoncavallo’s Pagliacci. The singers, dressed as Italian villagers, sit and embrace as they perform. The painted backdrop and scenery depict a country village complete with rolling hills and trees, as well as a brick tavern. The set is completed by a secondary stage set up for Pagliaccio’s troupe. This secondary stage resembles a Greek temple and is positioned downstage left, its interior covered by drapery. A drum rests next to the stage. Musicians are viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground.

description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera singers Natalie Garrotto  as Rosina, and an unidentified performer portraying Figaro in, "The Barber of Seville." The singers wear18th century Spanish clothing and are surrounded by a stage set recreating a room in an affluent home. The set, painted with decorative paneled walls and a faux fireplace, is also decorated with end tables, chairs, and candelabras. The conductor stands in the orchestra pit near the foot of the stage. A pianist is visible to his left. Performed at John Adams Junior High School, Santa Monica.
A notice for this production of The Barber of Seville, performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at John Adams Junior High School, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 11 Dec. 1955: E5

description_tesim: Students pictured seated at typewriters, working on the school newspaper, The SaMoJaC. The students wear 1930s era clothing. The young man at foreground holds on open paper with one hand and types with the other. The newspaper’s name and logo are displayed on a placard just beyond him. One male and two females work together on a story while the remaining students work alone at their typewriters.
The SaMoJaC, Santa Monica College’s first newspaper, was first published in 1930. It is currently named The Corsair.

One male and two females work together on a story while the remaining students work alone at their typewriters.
The SaMoJaC, Santa Monica College’s first newspaper, was first published in 1930. It is currently named The Corsair.

title_tesim: Political cartoon featuring the “General Pete Monkey Circus,” commentary on Santa Monica Bay drilling, 1954
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Cartoons, 1954. Oil. Montana Ave Laundries. Oil.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The cartoon references “Gen’l Pete” – shorthand for General Petroleum Corporation. In 1954, a contractual agreement was made between General Petroleum and the Santa Monica City Council in which the oil concern was granted a 10-year lease on Santa Monica tidelands. General Petroleum offered the city $500,000 and 35% royalties from recovered oil. The contract was dependent on a voter referendum that would change pre-existing city ordinances, thus allowing for oil drilling in city-owned tidelands. The amendment proposal lost by a 3:1 margin. In addition, Bartlett references the Civic Auditorium (on the building at top right), likely referring to a $2,895,000 bond issue also up for referendum. The bond was approved by voters.

Photograph of a political cartoon drawn by Adelbert Bartlett. The drawing lampooning General Petroleum and Santa Monica city management is set within the “Gen’l Pete Monkey Circus.” A building reminiscent of a fun house is depicted at the top, under text reading: “Voters! Think Twice Before You Vote Tues. Nov. 2.”  The words, “Civic Aud[itorium]” and “Town Hall” are written at top. Speech bubbles emanate from the mouth of a monkey’s head looming above the entrance, stating, “We’re Goners- -Jump!” and “Divy up Oil Bonus.”  Monkeys in top hats and suits run out of the building with “Imperium II” written above its doors. Their arms are outstretched in the air and one is depicted performing a cartwheel. At right, the City Manager floats through the air, buoyed by a parachute. Above him is a large bag of money. The circus balances perilously on the back of one man, bent over, his torso covered by a barrel. He holds a balancing pole while walking a tight rope strung between the Palisades cliffs and a “mythical oil pool” surrounding oil derricks in the bay. Seated under a palm tree near the cliff’s edge is a figure playing an instrument. Text beneath the figure: “Pete’s Horntooter O Delusion,” followed by the emphatic statement, “Oil . Vote No.” The Pacific Coast Highway and other landmark structures are depicted at bottom, with the bay and coast line captured at center. The cartoon is signed “BART” in the left-hand corner.
For duplicate drawing see image ark no.21198.zz002d5q1z

title_tesim: "Die Fledermaus" production, Symphonies by the Sea, Santa Monica, 1965
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Negs. Fledermaus. 8/27/65 for #2 paper.
named_subject_tesim: Symphonies by the Sea (Santa Monica, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Opera singers performing in Act 2 of Die Fledermaus. Singers wearing late-19th century ball gowns and tuxedos interact while two females (portraying Adele and Ida) converse center stage; a male stands near them on the right. An additional cast member dressed as an attendant stands in the background atop a set of steps.  He wears a powdered wig, military-style jacket, breaches and stockings, and holds a staff. Potted plants create a backdrop for the open-air stage, and separate the set from the girls’ gym building seen in the background. Set props include garden tables, benches, and banquettes, and one visible lantern. Beyond the stage, one of two accompanying pianists can be viewed situated on a walkway separating the stage from a small reflecting pool (just seen at bottom right). Performed August 27, 1965, during the 28th annual “Symphonies by the Sea” event, Open-Air Memorial Theater, Santa Monica High School Campus.
Symphonies by the Sea was a free-admission annual symphonic and operatic season which started at Santa Monica High School, then moved on to Santa Monica City College.
Event noted in the article, "Symphony-by-the-Sea Programs Scheduled,"  Los Angeles Times , 11 July 1965: WS7.
combined_subject_ssim: Symphonies by the Sea (Santa Monica, Calif.)

description_tesim: Two female equestrians ride past a judge and horse show attendees at the Palm Springs Field Club. The equestrians wear show hats, riding blazers with numbered armbands, breeches and boots. They ride along a fenced-off track for the judges (one can be seen in the background holding a clipboard and wearing a fedora, sunglasses dress blazer and slacks). In the foreground, attendees view the proceedings, talking to each other and drinking refreshments. All wear hats; all women wear dresses; two men wear riding boots. Two of the women each have a dog on a leash – one terrier, one retriever.  One of the women speaks to a man (standing third from left) who holds a riding crop in one hand. He is possibly Owen Earl Coffman, a member of the Palm Springs Field Club board of directors.

title_tesim: Political cartoon commenting on oil drilling in the Santa Monica Bay, 1954
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Cartoons, 1954. Oil. Montana Ave Laundries. Oil.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of a political cartoon drawn by Adelbert Bartlett. Here, derricks gush oil into the tidelands and beyond. A small stick figure can be seen at the right of the oil slick, depicted yelling “HELP!” with its arms up. Beyond the oil tides at right are the Palisades Park cliffs and Santa Monica Mountains. At top, a marching band drum labeled “Santa Monica” is played by a disembodied hand. Text reads: “Vote No! Stop ‘Coal Oil Johnny!’” “Beat! Beat Oil. Off Shore Drilling. Ugly Pollution. It Ruins.” Text at bottom: “Stop Oil Graft…Vote No… Vote No.” Below these words is an image of a man wearing a top hat, smoking a cigar, and holding bags of money. He is “Juggler ‘Johnny’ The Coal Oil Kid.” More money bags sit beneath a table. Text above that reads: “Oil Agent’s Bonus Table Contracts.”

The caricature, “Juggler Johnny, the Coal Oil Kid,” is possibly a symbolic reference to John Barneson (d. 1862-1941), founder of General Petroleum Corporation. In 1954, a contractual agreement was made between General Petroleum and the Santa Monica City Council in which the oil concern was granted a 10-year lease on Santa Monica tidelands. General Petroleum offered the city $500,000 and 35% royalties from recovered oil. The contract was dependent on a voter referendum that would change pre-existing city ordinances, thus allowing for oil drilling in city-owned tidelands. The amendment proposal lost by a 3:1 margin.
Bartlett created a similar image with slight variations (see image ark no. 21198.zz002d5q4h).

title_tesim: Political cartoon featuring oil clouds over the Santa Monica Bay, commentary on oil drilling, 1954
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Cartoons, 1954. Oil. Montana Ave Laundries. Oil.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: In 1954, a contractual agreement was made between General Petroleum and the Santa Monica City Council in which the oil concern was granted a 10-year lease on Santa Monica tidelands. General Petroleum offered the city $500,000 and 35% royalties from recovered oil. The contract was dependent on a voter referendum that would change pre-existing city ordinances, thus allowing for oil drilling in city-owned tidelands. The amendment proposal lost by a 3:1 margin.

Photograph of a political cartoon drawn by Adelbert Bartlett. Charcoal drawing illustrates a cloud of oil floating above the Santa Monica bay. Text beneath the cloud reads: “Oil Cloud Over Santa Monica Bay.” Farther down the cloud at right is written “Venice” and “Del Rey,” reminding the viewer of other cities that touch the bay. Oil derricks are pictured in the waters at left, while drops of oil descend from text reading, “Offshore Oil.” A large bag imprinted with the word “Bonus?” is tethered with a label that reads: “By Order Moniker d’Oro, The Black Gold Kid.” This is held up by a cigar-smoking man wearing a top hat and suit, standing in a stream of oil. Remaining text reads: “Bondage. Pollution. Ruin. Vote NO.”
For duplicate drawings see image ark nos. 21198/zz002d5q51 and 21198/zz002d5q6j.

description_tesim: Photograph of a political cartoon drawn by Adelbert Bartlett. At center, bundles of laundry sit on shelves with “Will Call” printed above. To the left, a spinning vortex (perhaps a washing machine) appears to create soap suds. Machines sit just to the left in the background. The image is surrounded by text. Text reads: “-Mat’n –Ett. Sudsy Monkey Business. Montana Avenue Laundries. ‘You Bring – We Do’ – Commercial – They Violate Self-Service Permit. Must We Submit to Spot Favors, ‘See Me’ Zoning, Downgraded Home Values? ...Montana (Laundry) Avenue. Today’s Road of Out-Zone Laundries- Animal Breeders – Heavy Machine Shop – Heavy Auto Repairs – Home Owners: Protest to Your City Council – Press – Growing Industrial Invasion & Ruin Home Areas.”

description_tesim: Seven young ballet students of Julia Stuart photographed in a park. The girls pose at the edge of a man-made pond. Six of the girls wear Grecian-style costumes. At center stands a young woman posed as if readying herself to dive into the water. She wears a leotard with sash at waist, tights and ballet flats. The photograph may have been taken at Douglas Park (2439 Wilshire Blvd., Santa Monica).
Two additional photographs feature these seven students wearing the same outfits, performing/posing in a dance studio (image ark. nos. 21198/zz002d5wzb, and 21198/zz002d5x5x).

Julia Stuart is noted as a dance instructor in several Santa Monica directories, dates ranging 1947-1960. Addresses include 2721 Wilshire Blvd. (1954 directory), and 2315 Wilshire Blvd. (1960 directory).

title_tesim: Nelda Scarsella and Enrico Porta performing in “La Traviata” at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles, 1951
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Frank Pace. Opera Traviata, 9/15/51.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 18
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Verdi’s “La Traviata,” produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace, was performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, September 15, 1951. Several notices were published in the Los Angeles Times. A critique appeared in the article, “’La Traviata’ Staged on Verdi Anniversary,” 17 Sep. 1951: B8

Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera production of “La Traviata,” Act 2, Scene 1. Opera singer Nelda Scarsella portrays Violetta (wearing a gown with ruffled trim and floral pick-ups). She sings at center with Enrico Porta (in the role of Giorgio Germont). The setting is an exterior space at Violetta’s country home outside of Paris. A large tree, shrubs, and garden furniture complete the outdoor scenery. Musicians are viewed in the orchestra pit beyond the stage at foreground.

Cast members from the Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera with conductor and Association director. From left to right: Francesco Pace, Founder and Director of the Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera Assoc.; Tandy MacKenzie, who portrayed Manrico; conductor Francisco Camacho Vega, Mina Doerr as Azucena; Enrico Porta as the Count di Luna; Barbara Patton as Leonora; an unidentified man stands at far right.

title_tesim: International Sourdough Reunion attendees, Hotel Alexandria, Los Angeles, 1949
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 18
Text from negative sleeve: 2907. Yukon Society Convention 1949. Wm H Brown L.A.
named_subject_tesim: Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Male and female attendees of the 18th annual International Sourdough Reunion, hosted by the Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California. They are standing in a lobby, likely at the Hotel Alexandria, Los Angeles.

International Sourdough Reunion, Inc. is a non-profit association of fraternal organizations that share the pioneer history of Alaska and the Yukon in common. Each year, one organization hosts a convention of members from groups located as far north as Fairbanks, Alaska, and as far south as San Diego, California (with points in between). In 1949, the Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California hosted the 18th annual International Sourdough Reunion convention. Events were held at the Hotel Alexandria in Los Angeles. The four-day event (August 17-20) was noted in the article, "Gold Rush is On: Tales of '98 Relived at Sourdough Meeting," Los Angeles Times, 18 Aug. 1949: A2.
combined_subject_ssim: Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California

caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2701 P1 to 11. Santa Monica Junior College, 10/15/35. Handicrafts from Egypt. Carleen Andrews, Jane Riley, Naida Sparks.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2701 P 2
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of Santa Monica College student Carleen Andrews, seated, shown wearing a patterned keffiyeh secured with an agal, and a light-colored fabric draped over her torso and shoulders. Andres holds a double-strand string of beads with a yarn tassel ornament and embellishments. On the table before her sits additional craft goods from Egypt, including an embossed metal cup, a painted ceramic jar, pointed-toe leather slippers and more strands of beads. A stripped textile with a macramé-finished edge hangs on the right side. Photographed at Santa Monica College.

These strings of beads were used as props in photo shoots with actress Margaret Wells and actor/student Ray Smith (see image ark nos. 21198/zz002d5wb1, 21198/zz002d5wfk, 21198/zz002d487b and 21198/zz002d488v).

title_tesim: Ribbonwood tree within a rock outcropping, Ribbonwood Resort, Santa Rosa Mountains, Mountain Center vicinity, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2803. Ribbonwood Resort. San Jacinto Mts Via Keen Camp Post Office. Wn S. Howell Jr. proprietor. Photos of Ribbonwood Trees, 10/1/37. Dr. St. Louis Estes. Jean Myras.
Handwritten along edge of negative: 2803
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Santa Rosa and San Jacinto Mountains National Monument (Calif.)
description_tesim: Photograph of a Ribbonwood tree surrounded by rock outcroppings, and shrubs, with the Santa Rosa Mountains in the background. Photographed on the property of the Ribbonwood Resort.

The Ribbonwood Resort, located at the base of the Santa Rosa Mountains, was owned and operated by W.S. (Wilson Stout) Howell, Jr., d.1888-1967. Howell purchased 2000 acres of land at the base of the Santa Rosa Mountains from the Southern Pacific Railroad with the intention of farming, but when the Pines-to-Palms Highway opened in 1932, he decided to open a rest stop to capitalize on the traffic that ran through portions of his property. Soon after, he began to clear paths to trails leading up to the Santa Rosa Mountains and created picnic areas. Rustic-style cabins soon followed. By 1935, Howell’s land was a full-fledged resort. Howell’s intention was to offer a place where those in ill health could rest and recuperate in a natural environment. He believed that the mountain location helped him rebuild his health and that it could do the same for others. Howell sold his land in 1964. It was developed into the town of Spring Crest, which sits within the Santa Rosa – San Jacinto Mountains National Monument.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Rosa and San Jacinto Mountains National Monument (Calif.)

caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2803. Ribbonwood Resort. San Jacinto Mts Via Keen Camp Post Office. Wn S. Howell Jr. proprietor. Photos of Ribbonwood Trees, 10/1/37. Dr. St. Louis Estes. Jean Myras.
Handwritten along edge of negative: 2803 P
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Santa Rosa and San Jacinto Mountains National Monument (Calif.)
description_tesim: Photograph of a valley populated by trees, shrubs and rock outcroppings, leading up to a mountain peak. Photographed on the property of the Ribbonwood resort.

The Ribbonwood resort, located at the base of the Santa Rosa Mountains, was owned and operated by W.S. (Wilson Stout) Howell, Jr., d.1888-1967. Howell purchased 2000 acres of land at the base of the Santa Rosa Mountains from the Southern Pacific Railroad with the intention of farming, but when the Pines-to-Palms Highway opened in 1932, he decided to open a rest stop to capitalize on the traffic that ran through portions of his property. Soon after, he began to clear paths to trails leading up to the Santa Rosa Mountains and created picnic areas. Rustic-style cabins soon followed. By 1935, Howell’s land was a full-fledged resort. Howell’s intention was to offer a place where those in ill health could rest and recuperate in a natural environment. He believed that the mountain location helped him rebuild his health and that it could do the same for others. Howell sold his land in 1964. It was developed into the town of Spring Crest, which sits within the Santa Rosa – San Jacinto Mountains National Monument.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Rosa and San Jacinto Mountains National Monument (Calif.)

title_tesim: Crowds along Lick Pier, Venice , 1928
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2486 P 5
Text from negative sleeve: 2481, 5 negs. Ocean Park Pier. Crowds on pier.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Throngs of men and women on the sand and walkways next to Lick Pier. On sand, the area is populated with beach umbrellas shading people in varied attire – from 1920s era bathing suits and caps, to dresses worn with cloche hats and suits worn with boater hats, fedoras and caps. They stand and converse, recline under beach umbrellas, or walk amongst the crowd.  Others sit on benches and watch people walk along an adjacent pathway. Additional crowds line up along the pier walkway. Establishments in along the pier seen in background, from left to right: “Skee Ball,” “Your Photo in Your Bathing Suit,” “Frank’s Place,” “The Jumbo Parlor,” “Lick Pier Bath House,” and an ice cream parlor (name indecipherable). Behind these businesses stand the Rosemary and Dome Theatres, along with the High Boy Roller Coaster and Light House Slide (spiral coaster), accessible along Ocean Park Pier.
Lick Pier was mostly in Venice, Ocean Park Pier in Santa Monica (Cf. http://www.westland.net/venicehistory/articles/pickpier.htm). View is from Venice side.

title_tesim: Clara and Carolyn Bartlett with sailing ships docked in the harbor, Long Beach, 1930
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2516 P 4
Text from negative sleeve: 2516. Long Beach Harbor & Ships, 1930. Carolyn Bartlett child.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of Clara Bartlett, seated, and daughter Carolyn Bartlett wearing a white sailor suit and tie, on a dock at Long Beach Harbor. Two clipper ships are docked in the harbor behind them. Painted on the ship at left: “INDIANA LOS ANGELES;” at right: “YA… SAMPSON.” A flag with a single star is positioned beneath. An anchor hangs next to a figurehead at the ship’s prow. The figurehead sculpture is a male anthropomorphic figure wearing a Plains Indian headdress. The figure gradually morphs into stylized Corinthian leaves. A ship yard sits across the water with a sign reading “Marine Terminal.” The area holds numerous buildings and (presumably) a gravel mound with conveyor.

title_tesim: Room with paintings in an American naive style by Jane Wooster Scott at the Windemere Hotel, Santa Monica, 1954

funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Hotel Windemere was located at 1431 Ocean Ave. Built in 1909, it was demolished in 1962. John Lindsay, a southern California architect, owned the building from at least 1954 until its demolition. According to newspaper reports from 1954 to 1956, he and Jane Wooster Scott were a couple.

Photograph of a room with 5 paintings in an American naïve style. Signed "Wooster," the paintings are early works by Jane Wooster Scott (b. 1929). The paintings hang on a wall covered in striped wall paper above a sofa and end tables at th4e Windemere Hotel.

description_tesim: Typescript describes photographs of Eugene d'Orange, Scottish-Spanish poet and artist who lived in Santa Monica. He was photographed with two young women- Valeska Radd and June Diebold. Most photos show d'Orange telling Radd and Diebold their fortunes. All show d'Orange and Radd wearing the keffiyeh (an Arabic headscarf) on the beach.
May be the same person as Eugene Harry Drake, resident of Santa Monica.
Typescript document describes three related photographs (see "Relation" note).

title_tesim: “Merry Widow” production with Natalie Garrotto, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, possibly 1960
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 35
Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.
English language adaptations of “The Merry Widow” include several name changes. Here, names from the original Viennese work are noted in parentheses after the variation.

Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in “The Merry Widow,” Act 2. Singer in the role of the widow Sonia Sadoya (Hannah Glawari) stands center stage. She and the other female performers (including Natalie Garrotto portraying Natalie (Valencienne), seated at the table on the left) wear the traditional clothing of a fictional Slavic country, including kokoshnik-inspired headdresses, peasant blouses, full skirts with aprons and waist cinchers. The men wear tuxedos and military dress uniforms. Cast members stand and behind her as she sings. The setting is a garden at Hannah’s home. Scenery flats representing trees, a faux brick wall and a lattice-work structure all contribute to the outdoor setting. Additional props include a rattan table, chairs and settee. Above the set, three microphones hang visibly from behind a border curtain. Beyond the stage, musicians can be viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground.

description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in “The Merry Widow,” Act 1. A female singer in the role of the widow Sonia Sadoya (Hanna Glawari) performs at center stage while fellow cast members (portraying party guests) look on. The scene is a ball being held at the fictional Marsovian (Pontevedrian) embassy in Paris. The women wear evening gowns; the men wear tuxedos and military dress uniforms. An additional cast member dressed as an attendant stands to the far right. He wears a powdered wig, full sleeve shirt, waistcoat, breaches and stockings. Decorative set pieces with intricate floral designs flank a faux marble staircase. Visible stage props include chairs and a settee. Above the stage, microphones can be seen hanging from behind a border curtain. Beyond the stage, musicians are viewed in silhouette in the orchestra pit at foreground.
Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.
English language adaptations of “The Merry Widow” include several name changes. Here, names from the original Viennese work are noted in parentheses after the variation.

title_tesim: Building at Market Street and Trolley Way, Venice, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2821. Leona Bertrand. 7/1/37. Temple Fires Relighted. Arab costume. Portraits as seeress.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This negative was stored with images of Leona Bertrand (negative sleeve notes Bertrand as subject). Bertrand was a Cuban born dramatics teacher, residing in 1938 at 115 Wadsworth in Ocean Park. The city directory lists her as a dramatics teacher with Fern Severance (a drama, music and voice teacher). Severance resided (and most likely taught lessons) at 73 Market Street in Venice, the address of the building in this photograph. The building still stands at Market Street and Pacific Avenue. Its original brick façade has been maintained.

Photograph of a two-story building on the corner of Market Street and Trolley Way (now Pacific Avenue). No addresses or store names can be viewed on the building- only a sign reading “Furniture” and a “For Sale” sign hanging above a shuttered store front to the right. An entry way to the left of the furniture store probably leads to the building’s second floor. Two 1930s era cars are parked on opposing sides of the street. The building sits on a corner running perpendicular to a Pacific Electric cable car track. At foreground, signs mark the rail crossing and warn to “LOOK OUT FOR THE CARS.” Cable lines are seen above, with utility poles on view in the foreground and near the building.

description_tesim: This negative was stored with images of Leona Bertrand (negative sleeve notes Bertrand as subject). Bertrand was a Cuban born dramatics teacher, residing in 1938 at 115 Wadsworth in Ocean Park. The city directory lists her as a dramatics teacher with Fern Severance (a drama, music and voice teacher). Severance resided (and most likely taught lessons) at 73 Market Street in Venice, the address of the building in this photograph. The building still stands at Market Street and Pacific Avenue. Its original brick façade has been maintained.

Photograph of building at the cross-section of Market Street and Trolley Way (now Pacific Avenue). An entry way on the left (with chevron patterned brick work above the entrance) leads to the building’s second floor, where Fern Severance taught music and drama. A furniture store sits to the right of the entry, with furnishings on display behind its windows. A “For Sale” sign hangs above a shuttered store front to the right of the store. No addresses or store names can be viewed on the two-story brick building. Two 1930s era cars are parked along the adjacent curb, while a third car is viewed across the street at right. Additional businesses can be seen along Trolley Way (now Pacific Avenue), one marked with address number 1424. Pacific Electric cable car lines can be viewed overhead attached to utility poles seen along the street.

title_tesim: Leona Bertrand as a seer, Venice, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2821. Leona Bertrand. 7/1/37. Temple Fires Relighted. Arab costume. Portraits as seeress.

description_tesim: Bertrand was a Cuban born dramatics teacher, residing in 1938 at 115 Wadsworth in Ocean Park. The city directory lists her as a dramatics teacher with Fern Severance (a drama, music and voice teacher). Severance resided (and most likely taught lessons) at 73 Market Street in Venice. Bertrand was probably photographed at this location (see images ark nos. 21198/zz002d6k62 and 21198/zz002d6k83).

Salon portrait of Leona Bertrand dressed as a seer (clairvoyant). Bertrand wears a sheer white dress with gold embellishments at the neck, chest and waist. A veil made from the same gossamer material is draped over her head and shoulders - the veil secured by an elaborate band evoking an Egyptian crown. She gazes down into an urn, holding it at its sides. The urn is placed on a pedestal before her. Hanging on the wall in the background are curtains and an Oriental tapestry embroidered with floral designs.

title_tesim: Carolyn Bartlett beneath a palm tree on a Palisades Park cliff at Montana Avenue, Santa Monica, circa 1946
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Carolyn in Park, 1940s.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)
description_tesim: At some point after WWII the rustic branch fence at Palisades park was replaced with two types: a pre-cast concrete fence with a criss-cross design and a wood board fence. Bartlett photographs of Palisades Park dated 1946 show the rustic branch fence and the wood rail fence.

Bartlett photographed from this location several times. The collection includes two photographs of Carolyn Bartlett seated at this palm tree. In those 1946 photos, she wears the same shoes, a similar dark skirt, and a solid-colored jacket (image ark nos. 21198/zz002d4xwb, and 21198/zz002d4xt9).

Photograph of Carolyn Bartlett seated beneath one of two palm trees on the Palisades Park cliffs. A portion of the cliffs and wooden rail fence can be seen at left, with structures along Pacific Coast Highway (California Highway 1) and the Santa Monica coastline in view at right. Photographed at the foot of Montana Avenue, above the Sorrento Beach Club.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: “New Moon” production, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1949
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 14 New Moon
Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.

description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera singers in a performance of “The New Moon.” A group of female performers (wearing peasant blouses and full skirts) sing while male cast members dressed as sailors (in stripped shirts and light colored pants) look on. The singer standing front and center (wearing a bonnet) is likely portraying Marianne Beaunoir, the daughter of a New Orleans ship owner and planter. The setting is the deck of a ship named the “New Moon.” The captain’s cabin and ship’s helm are viewed at right, with rigging running diagonally toward the ship’s railing. The ship is set against a backdrop of painted ocean waves. Photographed from the balcony, with full view of the stage, the microphones placed at the foot of the stage, and a portion of the orchestra pit (seen in silhouette).
Negative housed with envelope addressed to Adelbert Bartlett. Handwritten script reads:  “New Moon, operetta, de Parker, 1949.” (See image ark no. 21198/zz002d6p71)

description_tesim: Cast members from a Santa Monica Civic Opera production of “The New Moon.” Male cast members wearing sailor attire (stripped pants and socks, light pants, short jackets) are seated and standing atop a stage set, while a female performer stands to the left in attire reminiscent of the late 1800s. They are photographed on stage with set props recreating a plantation servants’ quarters. Beyond the stage, an orchestra pit devoid of musicians is viewed at foreground.

title_tesim: “Elisir d’amore” production with Natalie Garrotto, Memorial Greek Amphitheatre, 1949-1958
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: SM Civic Opera, Pagliacci, Elixir, Cavalleria.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: June Moss was in this production. In the Adelbert Bartlett collection, June Moss is documented in Santa Monica Civic Opera productions from 1949 to 1958.

Opera singers performing in a production of “L’Elisir d’amore.” At center, a singer portraying Dr. Dulcamara stands on an elaborately decorated wagon, while is assistant, wearing military style jacket, stands near him at ground level. They are surrounded by cast members dressed as peasants and soldiers. Among them is Natalie Garrotto in the role of Giannetta (wearing a full skirt with floral embellishments, ballet flats and a white shawl). Most in the crowd hold bottles of Dr. Dulcamara’s elixir. The setting is a village square in Basque country, late 18th century. Scenery includes a painted backdrop with rolling hills and trees, and set pieces painted to look like stone architecture. At foreground, one musician can be seen near the edge of the stage. Opera performed at Santa Monica High School Amphitheatre.

title_tesim: “Cavalleria Rusticana” production with June Moss and Barbara Gholson, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: SM Civic Opera, Pagliacci, Elixir, Cavalleria.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Opera singers performing the Mass passage from “Cavalleria Rusticana.” Cast members in Italian peasant costumes take up the stage. Several men (including two soldiers) stand behind rows of kneeling women. A priest stands in the doorway of a church as a performer, portraying Lola, sings. The setting is a 19th century Sicilian village. Painted backdrops and sets depict stone and brick architecture surrounding a village square. At opposing ends of the stage are a cantina and a church. Beyond the stage, musicians are viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground. Cast members include Barbara Gholson (kneeling, front, L), and June Moss, (kneeling, second from R). Opera performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

Image housed with other negatives from a performance of “Cavalleria Rusticana” at Barnum Hall, May 11, 1952. This is a different performance at the same venue with similar sets. This performance may have been staged the same year.

Opera singers performing in “L’Elisir d’amore.” June Moss, performing as Adina, sings with a male performer portraying Belcore. She wears a peasant blouse, lace-up bodice and full skirt with apron. He and the other soldiers wear belted, double-breasted jackets and shako hats topped with plumes. Portions of background cutouts in the form of houses and trees can be seen behind the players. Additionally, two standing microphones can be viewed at opposite ends of the stage, while musicians sit at foreground just below the stage. Opera performed at Santa Monica High School Amphitheatre.

Opera singers performing in a production of “L’Elisir d’amore.” At center, Adina (portrayed by June Moss) sings with two unidentified males performing in the role of Belcore (in military uniform) and Nemorino (dressed as a peasant). Cast members dressed as peasants and soldiers stand in the background. The setting is a village square in Basque country, late 18th century. Scenery includes a painted backdrop with rolling hills and trees, and set pieces painted to look like stone architecture - one of which stands in for a cantina complete with a table and chairs. Beyond the stage, the conductor and musicians can be viewed at ground level, while audience members are observed in the stands at foreground. Opera performed at Santa Monica High School Amphitheatre.

description_tesim: Opera singers performing in a Santa Monica Civic Opera production of “Pagliacci.” Cast members sit stage right, making up the audience for a play-within-a-play. They watch as Natalie Garrotto, singing as Nedda/ Columbina, is being serenaded by Columbina’s lover, Harlequin. She stands on a secondary stage resembling a Greek temple. The stage’s curtains are pulled back to reveal an interior space with table and chairs. A banner painted with “I Pagliacci” is attached to the pediment. . Opera performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

title_tesim: Captain Baksey, (Santa Monica?), 1942
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Capt. Baksey. 20th St. c/o John Crum. 4/23/42.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: "[...]NIE A Caine" probably refers to the ship "Minnie A Caine," a four-masted schooner that was built in Seattle in 1900 by Moran Bros. for Charles Nelson of San Francisco. The ship was blown ashore north of Santa Monica in 1939 and was burned for her metal the same year.

Photograph of Captain Baksey, smoking a pipe and wearing a fisherman's cap, pull over shirt and pants. He stands on a set of concrete stairs against a dwelling with clapboard siding. A window and door are viewed on the right. Hanging from the wall behind him is a lifebuoy with words painted around the ring. Visible text reads: "[...]NIE A Caine" (probably "Minnie A Caine"). Captain Baksey holds a length of boating rope in both hands. Placed next to him on the stairs are three links from a large anchor chain, and a ship's navigation lantern.
Negative sleeve notes John Crum, a resident of Santa Monica. A number of Crum's memorabilia items were photographed by Adelbert Bartlett and are represented in this collection.
